hub.content_markdown:
# The Day Your Board Member Finds Out The School's Insurance Stopped Protecting Her

*A plain-English look at Side-A DIC coverage, the layer that protects an individual director's own money when the main D&O policy runs dry, gets refused, or never responds at all.*

---

A volunteer joins your board to help a school. Parents, a local accountant, a retired principal, people who serve for free.

Years later one of them is named, personally, in a lawsuit over a board decision.

She assumes the school's policy has her. It did. Right up until the moment it didn't.

Here is the moment.

---

## The reassurance every volunteer is given

Nobody agrees to sit on a charter school board expecting to bet their house on it. They show up because they care about the school, they have a skill the board needs, and somebody told them the school carries Directors and Officers insurance so they're protected.

That's usually true. The school does carry D&O, and most of the time it works exactly the way everyone assumes. A claim comes in, the policy defends the board, the volunteers never feel a thing.

The problem isn't the normal case. It's the handful of specific situations where a standard D&O policy quietly stops protecting the individual and starts protecting everyone but the individual. Those situations are rare, but they're the exact ones that pull a board member's personal savings into a courtroom. And there's a coverage built for nothing else but plugging them.

So let me walk through how a D&O policy is actually wired, where it leaves an individual exposed, and what closes the gap.

## The three sides of a D&O policy, plainly

A standard D&O policy is really three coverages in one, and the industry calls them sides.

**Side A** pays the individual directors and officers directly, when the organization can't or won't reimburse them. This is the part that protects a person's own assets. It usually pays from the first dollar, with no deductible, because it exists for the moment the individual has nobody else standing behind them.

**Side B** reimburses the organization when it does step in and cover its people. The school pays the defense, the policy pays the school back.

**Side C** covers the entity itself, the school as an organization, when the school is named as a defendant.

That structure is fine. Here is the detail that matters more than any other on this topic: all three sides almost always share one combined limit. One pot of money for the individuals, the reimbursement, and the entity, all at once.

Hold that thought, because the shared limit is where the trouble starts.

## Where the individual gets left exposed

Most board members never learn that "the school has D&O" and "I am personally protected no matter what" are two different statements. There are four ways the gap opens, and a school can hit any of them.

**The shared limit gets eaten.** Picture a serious lawsuit that names the school and several board members together. The school's own defense, the Side C piece, starts drawing down that single shared pot. Defense costs typically erode the limit rather than sitting on top of it, so a hard-fought case can burn through the money before the individual trustees are ever made whole. The entity and the individuals are drinking from the same glass, and the entity often drinks first.

**The school can't indemnify.** Side A exists for when the organization is financially unable to back its people. For charter schools that is not a hypothetical. Charter funding follows enrollment, authorizer renewals can go sideways, and a school under real financial distress, or one that has closed, may simply have no money to defend a former board member. Insolvency and bankruptcy are among the most common reasons an organization cannot indemnify. The board member who governed during the hard years is the one most likely to get named, and the least likely to have a solvent school behind them.

**The school is legally barred from indemnifying.** In some situations the law itself prohibits an organization from covering an individual, certain derivative suits and public-policy limits among them. The school might want to help and be told it cannot.

**The policy itself fails to respond.** The main D&O policy could be rescinded, voided over something in the application, or the carrier could refuse a claim or become insolvent. When the policy that was supposed to protect the individual is the policy that falls apart, the individual is the one holding the bag.

In every one of these, the entity has options. The individual volunteer does not. And the protections people assume cover them, state volunteer-immunity laws and the federal Volunteer Protection Act, are far thinner than they sound. They offer limited immunity from certain claims, but they generally do not pay the cost of defending a lawsuit, and they fall away for gross negligence or conduct outside the volunteer's role. A baseless claim still has to be fought, and fighting it costs real money.

## The layer built for exactly this: Side-A DIC

There is a policy designed to sit above the regular D&O program and do one job: protect the individuals when the underlying coverage runs out, refuses, or fails. It's called Side-A DIC. DIC stands for difference in conditions.

It does two distinct things, and both matter.

First, it adds **excess Side-A limit**. This is dedicated money reserved for the individual directors and officers, sitting on top of the main policy. When the shared limit underneath gets drained by the entity's side of a fight, this dedicated layer is still there for the people. The entity cannot touch it.

Second, and this is what separates it from a plain excess policy, it **drops down**. "Difference in conditions" means the policy fills the gaps where the underlying coverage does not respond. If the main insurer becomes insolvent, refuses to pay, or tries to rescind, or if the school cannot indemnify, the Side-A DIC policy drops down and pays in place of the coverage that failed. It steps into the hole rather than waiting politely on top of it.

Two more features make it the strongest personal protection a board member can have. These policies are typically written with far fewer exclusions than a standard D&O form, which means they respond in situations where the underlying policy would have denied. And they are usually **non-rescindable**, meaning once the policy is written the insurer cannot later void it over something in the application. For an individual director, non-rescindable is everything, because it removes the risk that a mistake someone else made on a form wipes out their personal protection.

One honest caveat. The exact triggers, exclusions, and drop-down wording vary from carrier to carrier, so treat all of this as the concept rather than fixed contract language. The purpose is consistent across the market even when the fine print isn't.

And to be clear about where this fits: Side-A DIC complements the base D&O and management liability program, it does not replace it. The main policy still does the everyday work. This is the safety net under the trapeze, for the day the everyday work isn't enough.

## Why this lands harder on a charter board

For a large company with deep reserves, some of these gaps are remote. For a charter school they cluster together in a way that is almost tailor-made to catch a volunteer.

The board is unpaid. Parents and community members serving for free are the ones whose personal homes and savings are on the line, and a D&O claim names those people by name. The money they're risking is their own, earned somewhere else, put at stake to help a school.

The financial fragility is real and it correlates with claims. The board decisions most likely to generate a lawsuit, closures, layoffs, authorizer fights, financial restructuring, are the same decisions that happen when the school has the least money to defend anyone. The exposure and the inability to indemnify peak at the same moment. That is precisely the moment Side-A exists for.

And the business office is lean. Most charters have no in-house risk manager to notice that the D&O limit is shared, or that there's no dedicated Side-A layer, or that a single rescission could unravel the whole thing. So the gap sits there unseen until a claim makes it visible, which is the worst possible time to discover it.

## Why the gap is usually nobody's fault but the broker's

Here is the part that should bother any board that has been coasting on "we have D&O."

A generalist broker, the kind who treats a charter school as a small account to renew quickly, sells the base D&O policy and stops there. The conversation about what happens to an individual director when the shared limit is exhausted, or when the school is insolvent, or when the policy gets rescinded, never takes place. Not because it's complicated to explain, but because raising it means doing more than processing a renewal.

The easy-button renewal rolls the same policy forward every year. Nobody pulls the structure apart. Nobody asks whether the individuals have their own protected limit or whether they're sharing one pot with the entity. Nobody mentions that Side-A DIC exists, because mentioning it invites a question the broker would then have to answer.

So your volunteers walk around believing they're fully protected, based on a policy nobody has examined from the individual's point of view. The reassurance is free. The gap is invisible. And the only person who pays for the silence is the board member whose name ends up on a complaint.

## What a broker actually doing the job would do

A broker who takes a charter board seriously starts from the individual, not the entity. They look at the D&O program and ask whether the directors and officers have limit that's protected from the entity's side of a shared-limit claim. They walk the board through the four ways a standard policy can leave a person exposed, in plain language, before anything goes wrong. They size a dedicated Side-A DIC layer to the real risk the school carries, and they confirm in writing that it's non-rescindable and that its drop-down terms reach the situations a charter is most likely to face.

That's the difference between a broker who treats your trustees like real people with real assets on the line, and one who treats the account like a file to clear before the deadline.

## Before you tell your next recruit they're protected

You don't have to take anyone's word for this, this page included. You just have to check one thing on behalf of the people who serve you.

So here's the question worth sitting with:

If your school went insolvent tomorrow and a former board member were sued personally for a decision the board made years ago, whose money defends her, and has anyone confirmed she has protected limit that the school's own legal bills can't drain first?

If you can't answer that with confidence, the volunteers on your board don't have a coverage problem yet. They have a question nobody has asked on their behalf. The check happens long before any claim. What it costs to skip it only shows up on the day someone you recruited finds out the protection she counted on was never built.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a second set of eyes on whether your board members are actually protected personally, and not just sharing a limit with the entity, that's the kind of thing we look at before anyone's name ends up on a complaint.*

topics.title: Everyone Sold Your School Cyber Insurance. Nobody Mentioned Crime. That Gap Is Where the Money Actually Walks Out.
topics.subtitle: Cyber and crime cover two different disasters. Most schools carry one and assume it does the other's job. Here are the losses each one pays for, the losses each one ignores, and why a school needs both.

hub.content_markdown:
# Everyone Sold Your School Cyber Insurance. Nobody Mentioned Crime. That Gap Is Where the Money Actually Walks Out.

*Cyber and crime cover two different disasters. Most schools carry one and assume it does the other's job. Here are the losses each one pays for, the losses each one ignores, and why a school needs both.*

---

A school I know was proud of its cyber policy. They had bought it the year it became the product every broker was pushing. Modern, responsible, box checked.

Then a longtime employee in the business office was caught moving money out over many months. Real theft, real dollars, the kind a crime policy is built for. They filed on the only financial policy they had, the cyber policy, and the carrier said no. Nobody hacked your network. Nobody breached your data. This is employee theft, and that is a crime policy loss. You do not have a crime policy.

One policy. The wrong one for that loss. Nothing to fall back on.

This is the pattern I see over and over. Cyber became the shiny new thing everyone sells, and crime, the older and frankly more boring coverage, quietly fell off the table. Schools end up with one of the two and assume the word "covered" stretches across both. It does not.

---

## Two policies, two completely different disasters

Cyber and crime sound like cousins. They are not. They respond to different events, and the losses one pays for are mostly invisible to the other.

A **crime policy** protects your money and assets from theft. Employee dishonesty and embezzlement, forgery and altered checks, stolen money or securities, robbery, and certain computer and funds-transfer fraud where a criminal moves your money. The through-line is simple: someone took funds from you.

A **cyber policy** protects against a network or data event and its fallout. Ransomware and extortion, a breach of student or staff records, the cost to notify families, regulatory exposure, and the business interruption when your systems are down. The through-line there: your data or your network got hit.

Notice that almost nothing on one list appears on the other. That is the whole problem. A school carrying only one of them is fully exposed to every loss on the list it skipped.

## Example one: the loss only crime pays for

Picture the embezzling employee from the opening, or a simpler version. A bookkeeper writes checks to a fake vendor for a year. Or someone forges signatures and drains a student-activity account.

Your cyber policy will not engage with any of it. No network was compromised, no data was exposed. From the cyber carrier's chair, nothing happened that the policy is built to see. This is theft of your own funds, and theft of funds is the crime policy's entire reason to exist.

A school with cyber but no crime eats this loss in full. And employee dishonesty is not exotic. A lean back office, trusted long-tenured staff, and limited separation of duties is the exact environment where it happens.

## Example two: the loss only cyber pays for

Now flip it. Ransomware locks your student information system the week before enrollment. You are paying to restore systems, you may owe notification to every family whose data was touched, and you are down for days.

Your crime policy shrugs. Nobody stole your money in the sense crime means. There was no embezzlement, no forgery, no robbery of funds. This is a network and data event start to finish, which is cyber's job and nobody else's.

A school with crime but no cyber absorbs the restoration bill, the notification costs, and the downtime alone. This is the rarer setup today, but it still exists, usually at schools that bought crime years ago and never added cyber.

## The seam that even both policies can miss

Here is where it gets sharp, because carrying both is necessary and still might not be enough.

There is a category of loss that sits right on the line between the two. A criminal poses as a known vendor and emails new banking details. Your business manager updates the file and sends the next payment. The money is gone that afternoon. That is social engineering fraud, sometimes called fraudulent instruction or deception fraud. A close relative, funds transfer fraud, is when a criminal fraudulently instructs your bank to move your money without an employee's involvement at all.

Watch what happens. The cyber carrier says no network was breached, your employee authorized the payment. The crime carrier says nobody broke in and took anything, your employee had authority and used it. Both denials hold up under standard language. The loss is real and each policy points at the other.

The fix for this seam is a specific endorsement, funds transfer fraud and social engineering fraud coverage, and it is its own line item. It can be attached to the crime policy or the cyber policy depending on the carrier, but it does not ride along for free on either one. A school can hold both base policies and still have this exact loss denied because the endorsement was never added. And when it is added, it usually carries a sublimit far below the main policy limit, which is its own thing to check.

## So what does a covered school actually carry

Three things, stacked.

**A crime policy with real limits.** It catches employee theft, forgery, and stolen funds. The losses that come from inside or from a thief taking what is yours.

**A cyber policy with real limits.** It catches ransomware, breach response, notification, and downtime. The losses that come from your network or data being attacked.

**The funds transfer and social engineering fraud endorsement, with an adequate sublimit.** It catches the wire-fraud seam that neither base policy will touch. Confirm which policy it sits on, confirm it is actually there, and confirm the sublimit matches the size of a payment your school could realistically send.

Add one habit to all of it. Any change to vendor banking details gets verified by a phone call to a known number, never the number in the email. Some carriers require that control before they pay, and it stops most of these losses cold.

## What the cyber sales rush left behind

The market spent the last several years selling cyber hard, and for good reason, the threat is real. But the same energy quietly skipped crime. A lot of schools were sold the new product, told they were "covered for cyber," and never asked the obvious follow-up: covered against employee theft? Against a forged check? Against a wired payment to a fake vendor?

A broker doing the full job does not hand you a cyber quote and call it financial protection. They put crime and cyber side by side, show you which loss lands on which policy, point out the seam between them, and make sure the social engineering endorsement is on one of the two with a sublimit that means something.

A generalist sells the shiny policy, says the word "covered," and leaves the older half of the picture blank.

## Before your next payment goes out, or your next system update

You do not need an audit to find your footing here. You need to answer three questions out loud.

If an employee quietly stole from your accounts over the next year, which policy pays? If ransomware locked your systems next week, which policy pays? And if someone emailed your business office posing as a vendor and a payment went out before anyone caught it, which policy pays, and what is the limit on it?

If any one of those answers is "I think we have something for that," that uncertainty is the opening. These losses are built to find the gap between the policy you bought and the one you assumed came with it.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want someone to lay your crime and cyber coverage side by side and show you exactly which losses each one pays for, that is the kind of review we run as a matter of course.*

checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Crime vs Cyber Coverage Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Crime and cyber cover two different disasters. Carrying one and assuming it does the other's job is how a school ends up with the wrong coverage for the loss it actually suffers.

A school filing on its cyber policy for an embezzling employee gets denied. A school filing on its crime policy for a ransomware shutdown gets denied. The two policies respond to separate events, and a third loss sits in the seam between them where both can deny. Here is which loss each one pays for, and the checklist we run on every program.

## Which loss each coverage pays for

| Loss | Crime policy | Cyber policy | FTF / social engineering endorsement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Employee theft / embezzlement** | Pays | Denies (no network event) | n/a |
| **Forgery / altered checks** | Pays | Denies (no network event) | n/a |
| **Ransomware / system shutdown** | Denies (no theft of funds) | Pays | n/a |
| **Breach notification to families** | Denies (no theft of funds) | Pays | n/a |
| **Wire to a fake vendor (deception fraud)** | Often denies (employee had authority) | Often denies (no breach) | Pays |

The crime column and the cyber column barely overlap. A school carrying only one is fully exposed to every loss in the other column. The bottom row is the seam: a fraudulent-instruction wire that both base policies can deny, which is why it needs its own endorsement. That endorsement attaches to the crime policy or the cyber policy depending on the carrier, it never rides along for free, and its sublimit is frequently capped well below the main policy limit.

## The coverage checklist

- [ ] Confirm the program carries both a crime policy and a cyber policy, each with real limits, not one standing in for both.
- [ ] Confirm a funds transfer fraud / social engineering fraud endorsement is actually on the program, in writing.
- [ ] Verify which policy the endorsement sits on; it can be the crime policy or the cyber policy depending on the carrier.
- [ ] Pull the endorsement sublimit and compare it against the largest payment your school could realistically send, not the main policy limit.
- [ ] If the sublimit is a small fraction of a realistic wire, request a higher one and price it.
- [ ] Put a verification habit in writing: any change to vendor banking details is confirmed by phone to a known number, never the number in the email.
- [ ] Confirm whether the carrier requires that callback control as a condition of payment.
- [ ] Document the confirmed policies, endorsement, and sublimit for the board record.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker sells the shiny cyber policy, says the word "covered," and leaves the older half of the picture blank. The cyber sales rush of the last several years did exactly that across the market: schools got the new product and were never asked the obvious follow-up about employee theft, a forged check, or a wire to a fake vendor. We put crime and cyber side by side, show you which loss lands on which policy, point out the seam between them, and confirm the funds transfer and social engineering endorsement is on one of the two with a sublimit that means something. That is the review we run as a matter of course.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

# The Day Your Network Hires Its Own Lawyer, It Creates a Risk None of Your Policies Were Built to Catch

*A plain-English look at Employed Lawyers professional liability: the coverage your existing program quietly leaves out the moment legal advice starts coming from inside the building.*

---

Your network grew. You stopped renting legal help by the hour and brought an attorney on staff. Smart move. In-house counsel reviews your contracts, reads your charter, advises the board, and keeps three campuses out of trouble.

Then one of those decisions goes sideways. A contract your lawyer drafted leaves the network exposed. A piece of advice turns out to be wrong, and it costs real money. Someone files a claim that points straight at the legal advice your employee gave.

You reach for your insurance, and you find out the policies you already pay for were never built to answer that exact claim.

---

## What actually changed when you made that hire

For years, your legal work came from outside firms. If one of them blew it, that was their malpractice problem, sitting on their professional liability policy. The risk lived somewhere else.

The day a lawyer becomes your employee, that risk moves inside your walls. Now the person giving legal advice is on your payroll, and the professional exposure that used to belong to an outside firm belongs to you. Most networks never stop to ask whether their insurance program followed the risk in the door. It usually did not.

This is mostly a larger-organization issue. A single-site charter rarely employs its own attorney. But growing networks and charter management organizations do, and the moment they make that hire, the math changes. Let me walk through why the policies you already carry leave a hole here, and what fills it.

## Three policies you already have, and what each one skips

Start with your general liability policy. It is the workhorse, and it is genuinely useful for slip-and-falls and the ordinary bodily-injury and property-damage claims a school faces. But every standard general liability form carries a professional services exclusion. That exclusion exists on purpose. It separates everyday liability from professional errors, the mistakes that come from giving specialized advice. Legal malpractice is the textbook example of what it carves out. So when a claim says your lawyer's advice was negligent, the general liability policy is designed to step back, not step in.

Next, your management liability or D&O policy. This one protects your board and your leadership from claims about how they govern and manage the organization. People assume it covers the general counsel too, and sometimes the general counsel does qualify as an insured executive. The trouble is the kind of claim. A D&O form is built to respond when leaders are sued for management decisions, not for practicing law. Many of these policies carry their own professional services exclusion, and a legal malpractice allegation against your in-house attorney can fall right into it. There is a second wrinkle worth knowing. The lawyers on your staff below the general counsel, the associates and contract attorneys doing the day-to-day legal work, often do not count as covered executives at all. They can be left out of the grant of coverage entirely.

Now the policy that sounds like the obvious answer: a lawyers professional liability policy, the malpractice coverage law firms buy. Surely that is the fit. It is not, and the reason matters. A traditional lawyers professional liability policy is built for a firm serving outside clients. Its entire architecture assumes the lawyer has clients who hired the firm, paid the firm, and can sue the firm. Your in-house counsel has one client, the employer who signs the paycheck. That is a different relationship, and the standard law-firm form is not written for it. Several states even exempt in-house counsel from the malpractice-disclosure rules that apply to firms, because everyone understands the in-house lawyer is doing something structurally different. Buy the wrong product and you have paid for protection aimed at a risk you do not have.

So here is the position a growing network can land in without realizing it. General liability excludes the professional act. D&O may exclude it too, and may not even name the junior lawyers. And the law-firm policy is built for a relationship your in-house counsel does not have. The risk walked in the door, and none of the three doors it tried to enter were the right one.

## The coverage built for this exact situation

There is a line designed for precisely this gap. It is called Employed Lawyers professional liability, sometimes just Employed Lawyers coverage. Its whole job is to protect the attorneys you employ for claims arising out of the legal services they provide to you, the employer.

In plain terms, it does the thing the other policies decline to do. It responds when someone alleges your in-house lawyer's professional work, the advice, the opinion, the drafting, the legal judgment, was negligent and caused harm. It typically covers the general counsel and the legal team beneath them, including the contract and staff attorneys a D&O form tends to skip. It usually advances defense costs, which matters because defending a malpractice-style allegation gets expensive long before anyone decides whether the claim has merit.

The cleanest way to picture it is as a deliberate exception. Where your other policies exclude the professional acts of a lawyer, an Employed Lawyers policy is written to cover exactly those acts. It is the patch made to fit the hole the standard forms cut out by design.

A few policies go further if you ask. Some can extend to pro bono work your lawyer does on the side, or to outside board service, when those activities are added by endorsement. If your general counsel sits on a community board or takes pro bono matters, that is worth raising specifically, because it does not come along automatically.

One honest caveat. These policies carry the conduct exclusions you would expect. Deliberately fraudulent, criminal, or intentionally unlawful acts are not covered, and they should not be. This coverage is built for honest professional mistakes, which is the risk that actually keeps a careful lawyer up at night.

## Why your broker never raised it

Here is the part that should bother you. This is not obscure coverage. Major carriers write it. Industry groups have published on it for years. Any broker who actually thought about your organization as it grew would have flagged it the moment you mentioned hiring an attorney.

Most never do. A generalist broker treats your charter network like a renewal to process, not a risk to understand. They run the easy-button renewal every year, roll the same package forward, and never ask the one question that matters after you scale: what changed about your operation since last year? Hiring in-house counsel is exactly the kind of change that should trigger a coverage conversation. Instead it sails past, because reading your operation and matching it to the right line of coverage is work, and clearing your file off the desk is faster.

The gap does not exist because the coverage is hard to find. It exists because nobody on your account connected a staffing decision to an insurance consequence. That is the entire job, and it went undone.

## What it looks like when someone is actually paying attention

A broker who knows growing charter organizations asks about your structure, not just your address. They want to know whether you employ attorneys, how many, what they do, and whether they touch anything outside their day job. Then they check your general liability and D&O forms for the professional services exclusions, confirm in plain language where your employed lawyers fall in or out, and tell you whether an Employed Lawyers policy belongs in your program.

None of that is exotic. It is the difference between a broker who maps coverage to how your organization actually operates and one who renews last year's package and hopes the year stays quiet.

## Before your next renewal

You do not have to take this page's word for any of it. You just have to ask one question and sit with the answer.

When your network hired its own attorney, did anyone on your insurance team look at whether the legal advice now coming from inside your organization was actually covered? Or did the renewal roll forward unchanged, while a brand-new professional exposure quietly took a seat at your conference table?

If you cannot answer that with confidence, you do not have a claim problem yet. You have a question nobody checked. The check is quick. What it costs to skip it does not show up until a claim arrives with your own lawyer's name on it.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools and networks across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If your organization has brought legal counsel in-house and you want a second set of eyes on whether the advice is actually covered, that is the kind of thing we look at before anyone asks.*

hub.content_markdown:
# Your Board Was Told The Umbrella Has Them Covered. The Umbrella Almost Never Does.

*A plain-English look at where board protection actually comes from, and why the commercial umbrella has nothing to do with it.*

---

A lawsuit names one of your board members by name. Personally.

Not the school. Her.

The board chair calls the broker, calm at first, because everyone remembers being told the school carries a big umbrella and the board is covered.

The broker goes quiet.

Here's why that umbrella was never there.

---

## What you were probably told

Somewhere along the way, a broker pointed at your program and said a sentence that sounded final. "You've got a sizable umbrella sitting on top of everything, so the board is protected."

It's a comforting thing to hear. It's also one of the most common misunderstandings in how a school's coverage actually fits together, and it's the kind of thing nobody catches until a director is staring at their own name on a complaint.

So let me walk through what protects your board, what doesn't, and why the umbrella you're counting on is built to sit somewhere else entirely.

## What "management liability" actually is

The protection your board is reaching for in that moment has a name. It's management liability.

Management liability isn't one policy. It's a category, a bundle of the coverages that respond when the claim is about decisions and conduct rather than a slip-and-fall or a fender bender. For nonprofits and private organizations it's usually sold as a single package combining a few distinct lines:

- **Directors & Officers (D&O)**, which protects the personal assets of your directors, trustees, and officers, along with the entity, against claims alleging wrongful acts in how they governed or managed. It pays defense costs, settlements, and judgments.
- **Employment Practices Liability (EPLI)**, for staff claims like wrongful termination, discrimination, harassment, and retaliation.
- **Fiduciary liability**, for breaches in how employee benefit plans get managed, things like imprudent investment choices or mishandling plan assets.
- **Crime/fidelity coverage**, often folded in or attached, covering employee theft, fraud, and the ERISA bond a plan sponsor may need.

That's the world your board lives in legally. None of it is bodily injury or property damage, which is the world general liability covers. Different exposure, different policies.

One more detail matters before we get to the umbrella. D&O is almost always claims-made coverage. It responds to claims made during the policy period, and it carries a retroactive date. Hold onto that, because it's part of why the umbrella can't help.

## Why the umbrella has nothing to sit over

A commercial umbrella does a real job. It adds excess limit on top of a specific, scheduled set of underlying policies. In almost every case those underlying policies are Commercial General Liability, Commercial Auto liability, and Employers Liability, the part of workers' comp that handles employee injury suits.

The key word is "follow-form." An umbrella follows the form of the policies underneath it. It inherits their coverage and their exclusions. It can only stretch a limit higher on a coverage that already exists below it.

Now line that up. If the policies under your umbrella are GL, Auto, and Employers Liability, then there is no D&O underneath the umbrella. No EPLI. No fiduciary. No management liability line of any kind. The umbrella can't sit on top of a coverage that isn't in its tower. There's nothing for it to extend.

On top of that, many umbrella forms carry their own exclusions for professional services, employment-related practices, and benefits-related exposures, which is the market's way of saying out loud that these risks are routed somewhere else on purpose. Industry guidance is blunt about it. An umbrella is not a substitute for D&O, and D&O typically needs its own separate excess policy when higher limits are required.

So when the board chair reaches for the umbrella, in almost every case it never had a thing to do with protecting the board. The word made it sound like a blanket over the whole organization. It isn't.

I say "almost always" on purpose. A bespoke or specialty program can occasionally be built to wrap management lines, and some package products combine coverages under one form. But the standard commercial umbrella does not, and assuming yours is the rare exception is exactly how boards get surprised.

## Where the extra limit actually comes from

If not the umbrella, then where? When a school needs more management liability limit than the primary policy provides, it gets built as a dedicated excess tower. Two pieces.

First, follow-form excess D&O, sometimes called excess management liability. These are layers stacked directly on top of the primary management liability policy, each one adding limit, each one written to follow the primary form. This is the real version of what people mistakenly think the umbrella is doing.

Second, and this is the piece generalists almost never place, **Side-A DIC** at or near the top of the tower. DIC stands for difference-in-conditions. Side-A coverage protects the individual directors and officers specifically, not the entity. It does two jobs. It provides additional dedicated limit reserved for the individuals, and it drops down to fill gaps when an underlying insurer can't or won't pay, like an insurer becoming insolvent or wrongfully refusing a claim, or the limit getting exhausted. The exact triggers and terms vary by carrier, so treat that as the concept rather than fixed wording, but the purpose is consistent across the market.

Why does that matter so much? Because of how a standard D&O policy is structured. It has three insuring agreements, called sides. Side A pays individuals directly when the school can't or won't indemnify them. Side B reimburses the school when it does indemnify. Side C covers the entity itself. And here's the part that bites: Sides A, B, and C usually share one aggregate limit.

Picture a lawsuit that names the school and several board members together. The entity's own defense, the Side C piece, starts drawing down that shared limit. In a hard-fought case, the limit can be eaten before the individual trustees are ever protected. Dedicated Side-A and Side-A DIC exist precisely to close that gap, giving the individuals their own protected limit that can't be burned by the entity's side of the fight.

## Why this lands harder on a charter

For most organizations this is a serious gap. For a charter school it reaches further, for a few reasons that are specific to how charters are built.

Your board is volunteers. Parents, local professionals, community members serving unpaid. D&O claims name those people personally, which means a trustee's own net worth is on the line for serving your school. The "you have an umbrella" reassurance is the cruelest version of this trap, because it tells volunteers they're safe while the coverage that would actually protect them was never placed.

Charters are people-heavy employers, too, which means real EPLI exposure. Budget-driven staffing decisions are a documented driver of rising employment claims at charter schools, and harassment, discrimination, and wrongful-termination suits are common across the sector.

Then there's the exposure that's purely yours. Some of the claims a school faces aren't about governance or employment at all. They're about students. Failure to educate, denial of special-education services, student civil-rights and discipline disputes. A generic nonprofit D&O policy may not contemplate those at all. The line that does is **Educators Legal Liability**, a hybrid of D&O and E&O built for schools, which responds to those student and parent claims and often bundles employment coverage with it. A board sitting on a plain nonprofit D&O can have a wide-open gap right here and never know it.

And funding pressure makes all of it worse. Charter money follows enrollment. Authorizer renewals, financial distress, closure decisions, these are exactly the kinds of board actions that generate D&O claims in the first place. The risk and the thin coverage tend to peak at the same time.

The quiet reason this slips through is structural. Most charter business offices are lean. No in-house risk manager to catch that the umbrella excludes management liability, or that the tower was never built, or that the form is the wrong one for a school. So the error sits there, invisible, until a claim makes it visible.

## What handling this properly looks like

This is fixable, and none of it is exotic. It's a placement done with care instead of on autopilot. Here's what to ask for and confirm:

Management liability placed as its own program, separate from the GL/Auto/umbrella package. A nonprofit or management liability package combining D&O, EPLI, and fiduciary, with crime where it fits.

The school-appropriate form. Confirm in writing that coverage reaches Educators Legal Liability exposures, the failure-to-educate and student-rights and special-education claims, and that the insured definition actually covers your trustees, officers, employees, volunteers, and committee members.

A right-sized primary limit, set with the knowledge that defense costs typically erode the limit rather than sitting on top of it.

A real excess tower when more limit is needed, built from follow-form excess D&O layers. Never the commercial umbrella.

Dedicated Side-A or Side-A DIC, so your individual trustees have protected limit that can't be drained by the entity's side of a shared-limit claim.

Fiduciary coverage carried deliberately, with the ERISA bond requirement for any benefit plan confirmed as satisfied.

And claims-made details reconciled across the whole tower: matching retroactive dates, prior-acts coverage, consistent terms layer to layer.

## What a broker actually doing the job would do

A generalist gives the board a sentence that sounds like protection. "You've got a five-million umbrella, you're covered." It's easy to say, it ends the conversation, and it's wrong in a way that won't surface for years.

A broker doing the job doesn't reach for that sentence. They show you, in plain language, that your board's protection comes from a management liability program and not from the umbrella, and they can point to exactly which policy responds when a trustee is named. They check whether there's an excess tower or just a thin primary. They ask whether Side-A is in place. They confirm the form actually contemplates student claims instead of assuming a nonprofit D&O is close enough.

That's the difference between a broker who treats your board like real people with real assets on the line, and one who treats your account like a file to clear before the deadline.

## Before you trust the umbrella again

You don't have to take anyone's word for this, this page included. You just have to check one thing.

So here's the question worth sitting with:

If a board member were named personally in a lawsuit next month, which policy would defend them, and has anyone confirmed it's actually in place, with its own limit, or did someone just point at the umbrella?

If the answer is the umbrella, you don't have protection there, you have a misunderstanding. The good news is that it's a placement problem, and placement problems get fixed before the claim, never during it.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a second set of eyes on how your board is actually protected, and where the limit really comes from, that's the kind of thing we look at before anyone's name ends up on a complaint.*

# LinkedIn Personal Newsletter: Management Liability Placement

*Spoke type: Personal profile newsletter (Aaron's voice). Goal: human story + compressed insight, driving to the full white paper. Post from Aaron's personal LinkedIn. ~750 words.*

---

**Headline:** Her board member got named in a lawsuit. The umbrella everyone trusted had nothing to do with protecting him.

---

A school leader walked me through her coverage a while back, and somewhere in the conversation she said the thing I hear constantly. "We're fine on the board side, we carry a big umbrella."

I asked her who told her the umbrella covered the board. She couldn't remember. Some broker, some renewal, some sentence said years ago that everybody just kept repeating. It had become a fact in her mind without anyone ever checking it.

So we checked it. And the umbrella, like almost every commercial umbrella, did nothing for her board at all.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where doing a job halfway meant somebody got hurt or something broke, and you owned it. That's the standard I expected when I got into insurance. The "you've got an umbrella, you're covered" line fails that standard, because it sounds like protection while leaving the people it names completely exposed.

Here's the mechanic, in plain English.

The protection a board reaches for when a director gets sued personally has a name. Management liability. It's a bundle, usually Directors & Officers, Employment Practices Liability, and fiduciary coverage, and it responds when the claim is about decisions and conduct rather than a slip-and-fall or a car wreck.

A commercial umbrella does a real job, but a different one. It adds limit on top of a specific set of underlying policies, almost always general liability, auto, and employers liability. And it follows the form of those policies. It can only raise the limit on a coverage that already sits underneath it. Since there's no D&O under the umbrella, there is nothing for it to extend. The umbrella was built to sit somewhere else entirely.

When a board actually needs more protection, that limit gets built as its own tower. Follow-form excess D&O layers stacked on the primary policy. And at the top, the piece generalists almost never place, Side-A DIC, which gives the individual directors their own dedicated limit.

That last part matters more than it sounds. A standard D&O policy has three sides that usually share one limit. Side A pays the individuals, Side B reimburses the school, Side C covers the entity. Picture the school and several board members sued together. The entity's own defense starts drawing down that shared limit, and in a hard-fought case it can be gone before the individual trustees are ever protected. Side-A DIC exists to keep that from happening.

There's a piece that's purely a school problem, too. Some of the claims you face aren't about governance or employment. They're about students. Failure to educate, denial of special-education services, student civil-rights disputes. A generic nonprofit D&O may not contemplate those at all. The line that does is Educators Legal Liability, built for schools, and plenty of boards are sitting without it and have no idea.

None of this is exotic. It's a placement done with care instead of on autopilot. The reason it gets missed is the same reason most of these things get missed: the generalist gave the board a sentence that ended the conversation, and nobody went back to check whether it was true. That's most of why Charter Select only works with charter schools and actually reads what's in the program.

I wrote the full breakdown on our site. No jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board or your business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If you only do one thing with this, do this. Ask whoever handles your insurance one question.

If a board member were named personally in a lawsuit next month, which policy would defend them, and has anyone confirmed it's actually in place, with its own limit? Or would someone just point at the umbrella?

If the answer is the umbrella, your board doesn't have protection there. It has a misunderstanding. And the good news is that a placement problem gets fixed before the claim, never during it.

Forward this to the board member who's been told they're covered. They'd want to know which it is.

Aaron Schwen

checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Board Protection Placement Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** "We have a large umbrella, so the board is covered." If that's the plan for your directors, the plan almost certainly has a hole in it.

A commercial umbrella adds limit over general liability, auto, and employers liability. It follows the form of those policies, so it has no Directors & Officers or management liability line to sit over. Real board protection comes from a separate management liability program, and higher limits come from a dedicated excess tower, never the umbrella. Here is how the pieces actually fit, and the checklist we run on every board-protection placement.

## Where board protection actually comes from

| Layer | What it does | What it is NOT |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary management liability** | The base program protecting the board and entity: D&O, EPLI, fiduciary, often crime. Responds to governance, employment, and benefit-plan claims. | Not part of the GL/auto package, and not provided by the umbrella. |
| **Follow-form excess D&O** | Stacked layers on top of the primary management liability policy, each adding limit, each following the primary form. This is the real source of higher board limits. | Not the commercial umbrella. The umbrella cannot extend a coverage that isn't beneath it. |
| **Side-A / Side-A DIC** | Dedicated limit for the individual directors and officers only, and it drops down when underlying coverage can't or won't respond. | Not shared with the entity, so the school's own defense can't drain it. |
| **Educators Legal Liability** | The school-specific D&O/E&O hybrid covering failure-to-educate, student-rights, and special-education claims. | Not always present in a generic nonprofit D&O form. |

A standard D&O policy's three insuring agreements (Side A for individuals, Side B reimbursing the entity, Side C for the entity itself) usually share one aggregate limit. In a claim naming the school and the board together, the entity's defense can erode that shared limit before the individual trustees are protected. Dedicated Side-A coverage is built to close exactly that gap.

## The board-protection checklist

- [ ] Confirm management liability is placed as its own program, separate from the GL/auto/umbrella package.
- [ ] Do NOT assume the umbrella extends over D&O, EPLI, or fiduciary. Confirm the umbrella's scheduled underlying policies in writing.
- [ ] Verify the insured definition covers trustees, officers, employees, volunteers, and committee members.
- [ ] Confirm the form reaches Educators Legal Liability exposures (failure to educate, student rights, special education), not just generic nonprofit D&O.
- [ ] Right-size the primary limit knowing defense costs typically erode it rather than sitting on top.
- [ ] Where higher limits are needed, build a follow-form excess D&O tower, not an umbrella extension.
- [ ] Place dedicated Side-A or Side-A DIC so individual trustees have protected, non-shared limit.
- [ ] Carry fiduciary coverage deliberately, and confirm the ERISA bond requirement for any benefit plan is satisfied.
- [ ] Reconcile claims-made details across the tower: matching retroactive dates, prior-acts coverage, consistent terms layer to layer.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker often ends the board-protection conversation with a single reassuring sentence about the umbrella, then never builds the program that sentence implied. We place management liability as its own deliberate structure, confirm the form actually responds to school-specific claims, and put dedicated limit where the individual trustees can reach it, because the people on a charter board are putting their personal assets on the line to serve a community.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at the claims-made retroactive date: the most expensive line of fine print most school leaders were never told to check.

hub.content_markdown:
# The One Date On Your Insurance Policy That Can Quietly Erase Five Years of Coverage

*A plain-English look at the claims-made retroactive date: the most expensive line of fine print most school leaders were never told to check.*

---

A claim lands in 2026 for something that happened in 2021.

You were insured the whole time. You renewed every year, paid every premium, never lapsed, never got a warning.

You're still not covered.

Here's the date that did it.

---

## The thing nobody walks you through

You run a school. You're balancing a board, a budget, a building, two hundred families, and a staff who need their benefits to actually work. When the insurance renewal shows up once a year, you do the rational thing. You skim the premium, check that it didn't jump too hard, and sign.

That's the exact behavior this whole problem is built on.

The number that matters most on some of your most important policies isn't the premium. It's a single date sitting in the declarations page, called the retroactive date. The moment it moves the wrong way, you can lose coverage for years you already paid for, and you won't find out until a claim shows up.

So let me walk you through it. What it means, then what it looks like when it goes wrong, then how to stop it.

## What "claims-made" actually means

Some of your insurance is occurrence coverage. Occurrence is simple and forgiving. If the bad thing happened while the policy was in force, you're covered, even if the claim doesn't get filed until years later. The year the event happened is the year that pays, and once you've bought it, that year is locked in for good.

A lot of the policies that protect you where you're most exposed don't work that way. They're claims-made. That usually includes:

- Directors & Officers (D&O), which protects your board and leadership
- Employment Practices Liability (EPLI), for wrongful termination, discrimination, and harassment claims from staff
- Educators' professional liability
- Abuse and molestation coverage, when it's written claims-made

Claims-made runs on a different logic. It covers a claim based on the year the claim gets reported, not the year the event happened. The claim has to be filed while the policy is active.

So far that sounds reasonable. The part that rarely gets explained is this: a claims-made policy only covers events that happened on or after one specific date. Anything before that date is excluded, no matter how many years you've been a paying customer.

That date is the retroactive date.

## The retroactive date, plainly

Your retroactive date is the earliest date a covered event can have happened and still get paid. Move that date forward, and everything before it drops out of your coverage.

When you first buy claims-made coverage, the retro date usually gets set to that day. Fair enough. But if you keep claims-made coverage running year after year, the entire point is to keep that original retro date frozen where it is. Every year you renew, your covered window stretches further back. Five straight years of coverage should mean five years of protected history behind you.

It should, anyway.

## How it goes wrong

The renewal cycle comes around, and a generalist broker, the kind who treats charter schools as too small to fuss over, goes shopping to knock a few thousand dollars off your premium. They find a cheaper carrier. They move your policy. They tell you they saved you money.

What they often skip, because checking it is tedious and they're in a hurry, is what the new carrier set as your retroactive date.

When a new claims-made carrier writes your policy, the default is to set the retro date to the day the new policy starts. Not your original date. The new one.

In a single renewal, your protected history can collapse from five years down to nothing. Every act before the switch, every decision your board made, every personnel call, every incident that hasn't surfaced yet, now sits outside the policy. You're paying about the same premium for a fraction of the protection, and the paperwork looks completely normal on its face.

The premium dropped four thousand dollars. The coverage you actually bought dropped by years. And nobody mentioned it to the board.

## Why this hits schools harder than anyone

For most businesses, an advanced retro date is a rough day. For a school, it can take the whole institution down.

It comes down to the kind of claims you face. The most catastrophic exposures a school carries, like abuse and molestation, civil rights, and certain employment matters, are also the ones with the longest gap between when the event happened and when the claim gets reported. Abuse claims especially tend to surface years, sometimes decades, after the fact, because of how and when victims come forward.

Now stack those two facts on top of each other:

1. Your worst claims are the ones most likely to be reported years late.
2. A claims-made policy with an advanced retro date specifically refuses to cover events from the years before the switch.

So the retroactive date trap doesn't put your small claims at risk. It puts the exact category of claim that bankrupts schools and pulls board members' personal assets into a courtroom. It lands on your single biggest vulnerability, and it gets set off by an ordinary money-saving carrier switch that reads like good news on the invoice.

## The fix isn't complicated. It just has to get done.

This is a solvable problem. It gets ignored because solving it means somebody has to actually read the policy, line by line, every renewal. Here's what protecting your retro date takes:

Keep the original retroactive date. When you switch carriers, the new policy's retro date should match your original date, not the new start date. You ask for that, and you get it confirmed in writing. It does not happen on its own.

Get prior acts coverage, sometimes called "nose." A new carrier can agree to cover events going back to your old retro date. That's the bridge that keeps your history intact through a switch.

Or buy tail coverage from the carrier you're leaving. An Extended Reporting Period, the "tail," lets you keep reporting old-event claims to the old carrier after that policy ends. A preserved retro date, nose, or tail: you need at least one of them working every time a claims-made policy changes hands.

Read the declarations page at every renewal. The retro date lives there. It takes two minutes to find, and it's the highest-stakes line on the page.

That's the whole fix. The reason it doesn't happen isn't that it's hard. It's that the broker on your account decided your school wasn't worth the two minutes.

## What it looks like when someone's actually doing the job

A broker who takes this seriously does three things you've probably never gotten from a generalist.

They renew early, with weeks of runway, so there's actual time to look at the structure of a carrier switch instead of rubber-stamping it the night before the policy expires.

They pull the claims-made policies and check the retro date at every renewal, and they show you, in plain language, what it is and whether it moved.

They treat your board's personal protection like it matters, because the people sitting on a charter school board are putting their own assets on the line to serve a community, and an advanced retro date can leave them hanging personally.

None of that is exotic. It's the difference between a broker who treats your school like a real client and one who treats it like a file to clear off the desk.

## Before you renew anything

You don't have to take anyone's word for this, including this page. You just have to look at one thing.

So here's the question worth sitting with:

When was the last time anyone actually pulled your claims-made policies and confirmed, in writing, whether the retroactive date moved at your last carrier switch? Or did the renewal just process itself while everyone was busy running a school?

If you can't answer that with confidence, you don't have a coverage problem yet. You have a question nobody's checked. The check takes two minutes. What it costs to skip it doesn't show up until the worst possible day.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a second set of eyes on your claims-made policies before your next renewal, that's the kind of thing we do before anyone asks.*

Her generalist broker had shopped it, moved carriers, saved her a few thousand dollars, and sent over the paperwork. She signed it the way all of us sign things during a week that's already on fire.

She found out the way no one should. A claim came in for something from years earlier, the kind of claim that takes a long time to surface. She'd been insured the whole time. She still wasn't covered. One line of fine print did that, and it got moved without anyone walking her through what it meant.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where if you did a job halfway, somebody got hurt or something broke, and you owned it. That's the standard I expected when I got into insurance. It is not the standard a lot of charter schools are getting, and this is the clearest example I know.

A claims-made policy only pays for events that happened on or after one specific date: the retroactive date. Keep that date frozen in place year after year and your protected history keeps stretching back. That's the whole point of staying continuously covered.

When a broker switches you to a new carrier to shave premium, the new carrier's default is to set your retro date to the day the new policy starts. Not your original date. The new one. In one renewal, five years of protected history can collapse to zero, and the paperwork looks completely normal.

- Or carry "nose" coverage, where the new carrier covers events back to your old date.
- Or buy "tail" coverage from the carrier you're leaving.
- And read the declarations page every renewal. The retro date lives there. It takes two minutes.

The reason this gets missed isn't that it's hard. It's that the broker decided the school wasn't worth the two minutes. That's the part I can't stomach, and it's most of why Charter Select only works with charter schools and reads the whole policy every single renewal.

I wrote the full breakdown up on our site, no jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board or your business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If they can't answer that clearly, you just learned something about both your coverage and your broker.

Forward this to the school leader who needs it. This one's too expensive to find out the hard way.

# Company Page Spoke: Claims-Made Carrier Switch Coverage Protection Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Switching carriers on a claims-made policy? Confirm these before you sign, or you may be paying full premium for a fraction of your coverage history.

When a charter school moves carriers on claims-made coverage (D&O, EPLI, educators' professional, abuse and molestation where applicable), one detail decides whether years of prior coverage survive the switch: the retroactive date. Advance it, and every event before the new policy's start date falls outside coverage. Here is how the three protection mechanisms compare, and the checklist we run on every claims-made renewal.

## The three ways to protect your coverage history

| Mechanism | What it does | Who provides it | When you use it |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Preserved retroactive date** | New policy keeps your *original* retro date, so prior years stay covered | Incoming carrier | Standard ask on any carrier switch. Confirm in writing. |
| **Nose / Prior Acts coverage** | Incoming carrier agrees to cover events going back to your old retro date | Incoming carrier | When the new carrier won't simply match the original date |
| **Tail / Extended Reporting Period (ERP)** | Outgoing carrier lets you keep reporting prior-event claims after the policy ends | Outgoing carrier | When the incoming carrier won't cover prior acts at all |

You need at least one of these working every time a claims-made policy changes hands. Zero of them is the gap that erases your history.

## The renewal checklist

- [ ] Identify every claims-made policy in the program (don't assume; verify the form basis on each).
- [ ] Pull the current retroactive date on each claims-made policy, in writing.
- [ ] On any carrier switch, confirm the incoming retro date matches your original date, not the new inception date.
- [ ] If it can't match, confirm prior acts ("nose") coverage back to the original date.
- [ ] If nose isn't available, price and bind tail coverage (ERP) from the outgoing carrier before the old policy lapses.
- [ ] Read the declarations page and confirm the retro date in writing before binding.
- [ ] Document the confirmed retro date for the board record.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker chasing a lower premium often sets the new policy up on the carrier's default terms, which advances the retro date silently. We renew early, read the full policy on every renewal, and confirm the retro date in writing every time, because the cost of getting this wrong lands on the exact exposure a school can least afford to lose.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

topics.title: The Claim No School Leader Wants to Picture Is Often Protected by the Smallest Number on the Policy
topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at sexual abuse and molestation coverage, why it is so often buried as a tiny sublimit inside general liability, and what real protection for this exposure looks like.

hub.content_markdown:
# The Claim No School Leader Wants to Picture Is Often Protected by the Smallest Number on the Policy

*A plain-English look at sexual abuse and molestation coverage, why it is so often buried as a tiny sublimit inside general liability, and what real protection for this exposure looks like.*

---

There is one category of claim that can end a charter school. Not strain it. End it.

It is also the claim most leaders never want to say out loud, so it rarely gets discussed at renewal. And because nobody brings it up, the coverage for it tends to be the thinnest, most overlooked figure on the entire policy.

Let me walk through this carefully, because the subject deserves care, and the mechanics deserve to be correct.

---

## Why this exposure exists at all

Any organization that brings adults and children together carries some risk of abuse. That is simply the nature of the work. Schools supervise children for hours a day, across classrooms, hallways, bathrooms, locker rooms, buses, field trips, and after-hours programs. The exposure is not a sign that something is wrong with a school. It is a structural reality of caring for kids.

Treating that reality seriously is not pessimism. It is the responsible posture, the same way a hospital plans for infection or a bank plans for fraud. The goal is twofold: do everything reasonable to prevent harm, and make sure that if an allegation ever surfaces, the school can respond, defend itself fairly, and stand behind any survivor without the financial side of it threatening the institution's survival.

Both halves of that goal touch insurance. And that is where most schools have a quiet problem they have never been shown.

## Why these claims are among the most severe a school can face

Three things make abuse claims uniquely dangerous to a school's finances.

First, the dollar figures. Settlements and verdicts in abuse cases can run very high, often well beyond what comparable claims in other categories reach, because of the lasting harm involved and the way juries weigh it.

Second, the timeline. Abuse allegations frequently surface years, sometimes decades, after the events in question, because of how and when survivors are able to come forward. Many states have widened or reopened the windows for these claims to be filed. So the exposure is what insurers call long-tail. The claim that arrives next year may concern a period long past.

Third, the cost of simply defending. Even an allegation that is ultimately found to have no merit must be investigated and defended, and that legal work is expensive on its own. A school does not get to choose whether to respond. It has to.

Put those together and you have a claim type that is severe, slow to surface, costly to defend, and capable of pulling in multiple claimants at once. This is exactly the kind of exposure insurance is supposed to be built around.

## How the coverage is usually structured, in plain English

Coverage for this risk is generally referred to as Sexual Abuse and Molestation coverage, shortened to SAM, or sometimes SAML or sexual misconduct liability. It tends to show up in one of three ways.

**As a small sublimit inside the general liability policy.** This is the most common, and the most quietly dangerous. The school's general liability policy carries a large limit, and tucked inside it is a separate, much smaller cap that applies specifically to abuse claims. The headline limit might be a million or more, while the abuse sublimit is a fraction of that. Many general liability carriers are reluctant to take on this exposure at all, so when they include it, they restrict it to a low figure.

**As an outright exclusion.** Some general liability policies simply carve abuse and molestation out entirely. The coverage that everyone assumes is there is, in plain reading, not there.

**As standalone coverage, or a properly built endorsement.** Here the abuse exposure is insured on its own terms, with meaningful limits, broader policy language, and defense built to handle this specific kind of claim.

The trap is the first one. A school reads "general liability, $1 million" and feels covered for everything underneath it. The abuse sublimit hiding inside might be a small slice of that, and against the size these claims can reach, a small slice can be the difference between an insured event and an institutional collapse.

## What adequate coverage actually looks like

A few features separate real protection from a box that was technically checked.

**Meaningful limits, structured for more than one claimant.** Properly built SAM coverage typically carries both a per-victim limit and an aggregate limit. The per-victim figure applies to each individual claimant. The aggregate is the most the policy will pay across all claims in the period. That structure matters, because these situations can involve more than one person, and a single shared pool can be drained by one severe claim before others are addressed.

**Defense costs that sit outside the limit.** This one is easy to miss and enormously important. When defense costs are paid inside the limit, every dollar spent on lawyers reduces the dollars left to resolve the claim. Given how expensive defending these matters can be, defense inside the limit can quietly consume the coverage. Stronger policies pay defense outside the limit, so the full limit remains available for resolution.

**Attention to how the policy is triggered.** Some SAM coverage is written claims-made, meaning it responds based on when the claim is reported rather than when the event occurred. Some is written occurrence, meaning it responds based on when the event happened, regardless of when the claim eventually surfaces. Because abuse claims are reported so long after the fact, the trigger and, for claims-made policies, the retroactive date are not technicalities. They decide whether a claim about a past period is covered at all. This deserves a deliberate conversation, not a default setting.

## The part that surprises people: the underwriting makes the school safer

Here is the quiet upside of insuring this exposure properly. To write meaningful SAM coverage, an underwriter generally wants to see real abuse-prevention controls in place. So the act of getting good coverage tends to push the school toward the practices that reduce the chance of harm in the first place.

The controls underwriters commonly look for are the same ones child-safety experts recommend:

- Background checks and screening on employees, contractors, and volunteers, including checks against sex-offender registries, refreshed on a regular schedule rather than just at hire.
- A written abuse-prevention policy that everyone is trained on and held to.
- Recurring, documented staff training on recognizing warning signs, maintaining appropriate boundaries, and reporting.
- A two-adult rule, so children are not left alone with a single adult where it can reasonably be avoided.
- Clear reporting procedures that follow mandatory-reporting law, with documentation of how reports are handled.

Schools with stronger controls generally see better terms and pricing, because they genuinely are lower risk. So the coverage and the prevention reinforce each other. Buying the protection seriously tends to make the underlying harm less likely. Few lines of insurance work that way.

## Why the gap is usually the broker's doing

So how does a school end up with a tiny abuse sublimit, or an exclusion, and never know? Almost always the same way.

A generalist broker, the kind who treats a charter school as a small account to process quickly, builds the program off a standard package. The abuse sublimit comes in at whatever the package defaults to, often a low figure, sometimes an exclusion. The broker does not raise it, because raising it means starting a hard, uncomfortable conversation, and the easy-button renewal does not require one. Year after year the policy renews on autopilot. The smallest, most consequential number on the page is never examined, never explained, never benchmarked against what these claims actually cost.

It is not that the school chose to underinsure this risk. It is that nobody put the choice in front of them. The default got accepted because checking it required someone to slow down and bring up the one topic generalists most prefer to skip.

A broker who actually knows schools does the opposite. They name the exposure plainly and respectfully, they pull the policy and read the abuse provisions out loud in plain language, they show the sublimit against what these claims realistically reach, they walk through whether it is sublimit, standalone, or excluded, and they connect the coverage conversation to the school's prevention practices so both get stronger together.

## The question worth sitting with

You do not have to imagine the worst day to take this seriously. You only have to look at one line.

So the question is this. If an abuse allegation surfaced at your school next month, possibly about a period years in the past, do you actually know what limit would respond, whether it sits inside your general liability or stands on its own, whether your defense costs would eat into that limit, and whether anyone has ever weighed that figure against what these claims really cost? Or has that line simply renewed itself, untouched, because it was easier for everyone not to bring it up?

If the honest answer is that you are not sure, you do not have a crisis. You have a line on a page that nobody has read closely. Reading it now, before it is ever tested, is a very different thing than discovering it afterward.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a careful, respectful second look at how your school's abuse and molestation coverage is actually structured, that is a conversation we are glad to have before anyone ever needs it.*
