topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at law enforcement legal liability for charter schools with a School Resource Officer, and the gap that opens the moment a sworn officer starts working your hallways.

hub.content_markdown:
# You Put an Officer on Campus to Make Kids Safer. You May Have Just Bought a Lawsuit Your Policy Won't Touch.

*A plain-English look at law enforcement legal liability for charter schools with a School Resource Officer, and the gap that opens the moment a sworn officer starts working your hallways.*

---

A student gets taken to the ground in a hallway. An arm gets twisted. A parent records the last ten seconds on a phone.

Eighteen months later, a federal civil rights complaint names the officer, the police department, and your school.

You call your broker, confident this is what insurance is for. Then you find out the policy you assumed covered it has language that carves law enforcement activity right out.

---

## The decision that quietly changed your risk profile

Bringing a sworn officer onto campus feels like a safety upgrade, and in a lot of ways it is. A School Resource Officer can build relationships, de-escalate, and respond fast when something real happens.

What rarely gets discussed in the same meeting is what an SRO does to your insurance exposure. The moment an armed, sworn officer is acting under color of law inside your building, your school is no longer just an educational operation. It is now adjacent to law enforcement, and law enforcement carries a category of claim that schools almost never see otherwise: use of force, an arrest gone wrong, a search that crossed a line, a detention a court later calls unreasonable. These get litigated as civil rights claims, often under the federal statute that lets people sue government actors for constitutional violations, commonly referenced by its code section, 1983.

Here is the part that catches school leaders flat. The way most SROs are structured does not keep that exposure off your doorstep.

## How SRO arrangements are usually built

There are two common setups, and the distinction matters less than you would hope.

In the most common arrangement, the officer is an employee of a local police department or sheriff's office and gets assigned to your campus under a written agreement. They wear that department's badge, answer to that department's chain of command, and are disciplined through that department.

In the other arrangement, the school employs the officer directly, sometimes through its own security or police function.

You might assume the first setup keeps you clean, since the officer technically works for someone else. It does not, at least not reliably. When an SRO uses force on a student, courts analyze it the same way they would any officer using force on any person, under the same constitutional standard. A plaintiff's attorney looking at that incident does not name only the officer. They name the department, and they name the school, because the officer was operating inside your walls, on your students, in connection with how your school runs. Whether the school ultimately owes anything is a question for the litigation. Getting pulled into the litigation is close to automatic.

So you can be drawn into a law enforcement claim even when you do not employ the officer. That is the exposure. Now look at whether your insurance answers it.

## What your standard policies actually say

A school typically carries two main liability lines. General liability covers ordinary bodily injury and property damage, the slip-and-fall side of running a building. Educators legal liability, often called ELL, covers wrongful acts tied to the educational job: failure to educate, discipline disputes, the professional decisions a school and its board make.

Neither one was built for law enforcement, and the standard forms tend to say so.

General liability is written for the risks of owning and operating a normal business. Law enforcement activity is a recognized professional exposure that gets excluded from these policies, frequently by a specific endorsement that removes coverage for the named insured's law enforcement activities. ELL has its own set of exclusions, and claims arising from intentional or criminal acts, the territory a force or false-arrest claim lives in, often sit outside it.

Read those two facts together and the gap comes into focus. The two policies you rely on most are each drafted to push law enforcement claims away. A use-of-force suit can land squarely in the seam between them, where neither one was designed to respond.

This is the line that built the problem: the coverage you assume protects you against an SRO incident is usually the exact coverage written to exclude it.

## The coverage that was actually built for this

The line designed for this exposure is Law Enforcement Legal Liability, sometimes shortened to LELL or sold as police professional liability.

It exists precisely because general liability shuts law enforcement out. LELL is built to respond to claims arising from law enforcement activities: excessive or improper use of force, wrongful arrest or detention, improper search and seizure, and the civil rights claims that get filed under that 1983 framework. It typically funds defense costs, which in a federal civil rights case can run high long before anyone reaches a verdict or settlement.

Like any line, it has its own limits and carve-outs. Coverage for the conduct itself generally does not extend to truly willful, wanton, or criminal acts, and these policies commonly exclude punitive damages. The specifics vary by carrier and by how the policy is written, so the structure matters and a careful read matters more. The headline, though, is straightforward. If a sworn officer is working your campus, this is the line that was engineered for the claim, and it is usually not riding along inside the policies you already have.

## The document everyone signs and nobody coordinates

When the officer belongs to a police department, the relationship runs through a written agreement, usually a Memorandum of Understanding, an MOU. This is the single most important risk document in the whole arrangement, and it routinely gets signed without the one person who should read it ever seeing it.

A well-built MOU spells out the officer's role, who supervises them, how complaints get handled, and, critically, how responsibility and insurance get allocated between the school and the department. The common pattern is that each party stays responsible for the acts of its own people, with each side agreeing, to the extent the law allows, to indemnify the other for claims arising from its own conduct. Many MOUs also state who has to carry insurance and at what level.

Sit with what that means. The MOU can obligate your school to indemnify the department in certain situations, or require your school to carry insurance for this exposure, and those obligations are only as good as the coverage standing behind them. A school administrator and a police chief sign the document. The school's broker, the person who would know whether the indemnity promise is backed by an actual policy, is often never in the room. The agreement gets executed, the officer reports for duty, and the insurance side of the deal stays an open question until a claim tests it.

Every path to this exposure runs through one place: the agreement that put the officer on campus and the policies that are supposed to answer for what the officer does. If those two are never lined up against each other, you do not have a program. You have a signature and a hope.

## Where the generalist broker let this slip

A broker who treats charter schools as small accounts to renew quickly is not reading your MOU. They were not at the table when it was signed, they did not ask whether a sworn officer now works your halls, and they renewed your general liability and ELL the same way they renew everyone's, without a thought about the law enforcement exclusion sitting inside both.

That is how the gap forms. Not through some exotic failure, but through the ordinary inattention of a broker who never asked the one question that changes the analysis: is there an officer on this campus, and if so, what happens when a claim comes out of what that officer does?

A broker who actually knows schools asks it on the first call. They want to know whether you have an SRO, how the arrangement is structured, and whether there is an MOU. They read that MOU against your policies. They check what your general liability and ELL forms exclude. And if there is a real law enforcement exposure with no line built to catch it, they tell you plainly, before a parent's phone ever starts recording, instead of after.

## The question worth sitting with

You made a deliberate choice to put a trained officer on your campus. That was a real decision about safety, and it deserves a matching decision about risk.

So here is the one worth asking yourself, today, while it is still a quiet question and not a lawsuit:

If an incident involving your officer turned into a civil rights claim next month, do you actually know which policy responds, and have you confirmed it isn't one of the two that are written to exclude it? Or has nobody on your side ever lined up the MOU you signed against the coverage you're paying for?

If you can't answer that with confidence, you don't have a coverage problem yet. You have a question no one has checked. The check is a conversation. The cost of skipping it shows up on the day you can least afford a surprise.

---

If you have an officer on campus and want a second set of eyes on your MOU and your policies before a claim tests them, that is exactly the kind of review we do before anyone asks.*

hub.content_markdown:
# Your Property Insurance Needs a Broken Window to Pay. The Worst Day at a School Doesn't Come With One.

*Why a violent event on campus can cost a school everything and still trigger no property claim, and the coverage built specifically to respond when there's no physical damage to point at.*

---

The unthinkable happens on your campus. There's no fire. No flood. No broken glass, no caved roof, nothing a camera would call damage.

In the weeks after, the costs come anyway. Counselors and trauma care. Round-the-clock security nobody planned for. Families who need support. A crisis communications team. And the quiet one that breaks the budget: students who don't come back, in a school funded by the students who show up.

You file a claim on your property policy. The adjuster asks where the physical damage is. There isn't any. Claim closed.

Here's why the policy you'd reach for can't help, and what actually does.

---

## Property coverage runs on physical damage. That's the whole catch.

A standard commercial property policy, and the business interruption coverage attached to it, is built around one trigger: direct physical loss or damage to the property. A fire, a burst pipe, a storm that tears off part of the roof. Something physically happened to the building, so the policy responds, and the business interruption piece replaces the income you lost while you repaired it.

That structure works fine for a flood. It falls apart for an act of violence.

A violent event on a campus can be the most devastating thing that ever happens to a school and cause almost no qualifying physical damage to the building. The harm is human, operational, and reputational. The policy that's supposed to keep you running after a disaster is looking for a kind of damage that this disaster didn't produce, so it stays shut.

## The bill that arrives after a violent event

Set aside, for a moment, the human weight of it, which no policy can address. Look only at the costs that land on the school as an organization, because those are the ones insurance is supposed to catch.

After a violent incident, a school typically faces some mix of:

- **Crisis management and communications.** Press, community, families, public officials, all at once, all immediately.
- **Psychiatric and medical care** for students, staff, and families, often for a long time.
- **Security upgrades and on-site presence** that didn't exist in the budget the week before.
- **Counseling and family support** at a scale a school has never staffed for.
- **Relocation or remediation** of the space, because sometimes people cannot walk back into the room.
- **Lost enrollment**, which for a charter school isn't a soft metric. It's the funding.

That last one deserves its own paragraph.

## Why this lands harder on a school than almost any other organization

Charter schools live and die on enrollment. Funding follows the student. When attendance drops, when families transfer out, when next year's enrollment softens because of what happened, that is a direct hit to the dollars that keep the doors open and the staff paid.

A normal business takes a violent event as a tragedy and a disruption. A charter school takes it as a tragedy, a disruption, and a funding cliff at the same time, with no corporate risk department and no cash cushion to ride it out. The very thing that makes the model work, money that follows students, becomes the thing that bleeds when students stop coming.

So the school carrying only standard property and liability is exposed exactly where it's weakest, and the coverage it assumed would respond is asking about broken windows.

## What actually responds: coverage built for the event itself

There's a category of coverage written specifically for this. It goes by names like active assailant, deadly weapon protection, or active shooter coverage, and specialty carriers now write it as a standalone policy.

The thing that makes it different is the trigger. It does not wait for physical damage to the building. It responds to the violent event itself. That single design choice is the whole point, because it's the reason it pays where your property policy can't.

A well-built policy in this category typically funds the costs that actually show up: crisis management and PR, medical and psychiatric care for victims, extra security, counseling, business interruption from the drop in attendance and enrollment, and remediation or relocation of the space. It puts money in motion in the days after, when a school has the least capacity to figure out funding and the most need for it.

## Reading one of these correctly matters

This is coverage where the fine print decides whether it's real, so a few things are worth confirming rather than assuming:

**The trigger definition.** Does it respond only to an actual event, or also to a credible threat that forces a closure and a response? The broader trigger covers more of what schools actually live through.

**What's inside.** Confirm it includes business interruption tied to enrollment and attendance, not just immediate crisis costs. For a charter school, the enrollment piece is the part that matters most and the part most likely to be thin.

**The limits, against the real cost.** Crisis response, long-term care, and a multi-month enrollment dip add up fast. A small limit is a gesture, not a safety net.

## Before you assume you're covered for this

You don't need to dwell on the scenario to answer the question that matters. You just need to know what your current policies actually do.

So here's the one to sit with:

If a violent event happened on your campus tomorrow and caused no physical damage to the building, which of your current policies pays for the counselors, the security, the crisis team, and the enrollment you'd lose over the following year? If the honest answer is "I'm not sure any of them do," then the gap isn't theoretical. It's the one your current broker never brought up.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want to know exactly what your current coverage would and wouldn't do on the worst possible day, that's a conversation we'd rather have now than after.*

# Company Page Spoke: Active Assailant Coverage Gap Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** A violent event on campus can cost a school everything and trigger no property claim. Here is which policy actually responds, and what to confirm before you assume you're covered.

Most school risk programs are built around physical damage. A standard property policy, and the business interruption coverage attached to it, only responds when something physically happens to the building. A violent event often causes catastrophic human and operational loss with little or no qualifying physical damage, so the policy stays shut. Below is how the relevant coverages compare on the trigger that matters, and the checklist we run when we review a school's exposure to a violent event.

## How the coverage responds to a no-physical-damage event

| Coverage | Trigger | Pays for crisis costs, care, lost enrollment? | Fits a violent event with no physical damage? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Commercial property** | Direct physical loss or damage to the building | No | No. No physical damage, no claim. |
| **Business interruption (attached to property)** | Income loss following a covered physical loss | No, the income loss must trace to physical damage | No. Enrollment drop without physical damage isn't a trigger. |
| **General liability** | Third-party bodily injury or property damage claims against the school | Defense and damages on covered claims only, not the school's own response costs | Partial and indirect. Doesn't fund crisis response, security, or lost enrollment. |
| **Active assailant / deadly weapon coverage** | The violent event itself, no physical damage required | Yes, built for exactly these costs | Yes. This is the coverage designed for the event. |

The single design difference that matters: active assailant coverage responds to the violent event, not to damage to the building. That is why it pays where the property program can't.

## The coverage review checklist

- [ ] Confirm whether the program carries any standalone active assailant / deadly weapon coverage at all, or only property and liability.
- [ ] Read the trigger definition: does it respond only to an actual event, or also to a credible threat that forces a closure and a response?
- [ ] Confirm the policy includes business interruption tied to enrollment and attendance, not just immediate crisis costs.
- [ ] Confirm covered cost categories: crisis management and PR, medical and psychiatric care, extra security, counseling and family support, remediation or relocation.
- [ ] Test the limit against the real cost: multi-month crisis response, long-term care, and a sustained enrollment dip, not a token figure.
- [ ] Confirm what the existing property and BI policies would and would not pay for a no-physical-damage event, in writing.
- [ ] Document the findings for the board record so the gap is a decision, not an accident.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker places property and liability, calls the program complete, and never tests it against the one event that produces no broken window. We work charter schools only, we read the full program, and we pressure-test it against the no-physical-damage event specifically, because that is the scenario where a school is most exposed and least able to absorb the cost. The enrollment piece is the part most likely to be thin, and for a charter school it's the part that decides whether the doors stay open.

topics.subtitle: A pre-loss claims-readiness playbook for charter schools. The claim you win is the one you prepared for before the building ever flooded.

hub.content_markdown:
# The Disaster Blueprint: Why One School Reopens in Three Weeks and the One Next Door Fights Its Carrier for a Year

*A pre-loss claims-readiness playbook for charter schools. The claim you win is the one you prepared for before the building ever flooded.*

---

Two schools take the same storm. Same county, same week, same kind of water through the same kind of roof.

One is back in session in under a month. The other is still arguing with its insurer the following spring, classes scattered across a borrowed church basement and two trailers, payroll bleeding, families pulling kids.

Same damage. Same coverage on paper. The difference between those two outcomes was decided long before the wind picked up.

---

## What actually separates them

People assume the fast recovery is about the policy, that one school bought better coverage. Sometimes that's true. More often, both schools had adequate policies and only one was ready to use it.

A property claim is a documentation contest. The carrier doesn't pay because the building is wrecked. It pays because you can prove what you had, prove what it was worth, prove the damage, and prove you did your part to limit it. The school that hands all of that over in the first week gets paid in the first month. The school reconstructing its records from memory while the mold spreads gets a fight.

You build the winning side of that contest before the loss, or you don't build it at all. After the alarm goes off is too late to start photographing equipment that's already underwater.

So here is the blueprint. None of it is expensive, and most of it is an afternoon of work you can start this week.

## 1. A current asset and property inventory, with photos and values

This is the foundation, and it's the one almost everyone skips.

You need a running list of what the school owns: furniture, technology, kitchen and HVAC equipment, science and shop gear, instruments, library and curriculum materials, playground and athletic equipment, anything bolted down and anything that isn't. For each significant item or category, capture a photo, a rough age, and a value. Serial numbers on the expensive electronics. Receipts where you have them.

Walk every room with a phone, closets and storage areas included. Video works too, narrate as you go. Then redo it once a year, because the inventory you took three years ago doesn't include the forty Chromebooks you bought last fall.

When a fire takes a wing of the building, the adjuster will ask what was in it. "A lot of expensive stuff" is not a number they can cut a check against. A dated photo and a value is.

## 2. Policies and the declarations page, stored off-site

Your insurance documents cannot live only in a filing cabinet inside the building they're insuring. If the building burns, so do they.

Keep a full copy of every policy, and especially the declarations page, somewhere the disaster can't reach. A cloud drive. A personal email to yourself. A folder your broker holds for you. The dec page is the one-page summary of what you bought: limits, deductibles, named insureds, and the coverage forms attached. In the first chaotic hours after a loss, that page tells you what you're working with.

Put the claims phone number and your broker's direct line in that same folder. When the office is taped off, you don't want to be hunting for a number on a sticky note inside it.

## 3. Know your deductibles and sublimits before you need them

This is where schools get blindsided, and it's pure homework you can do on a quiet afternoon.

Pull the dec page and read three things.

Your deductible. The amount you eat before coverage kicks in. Property deductibles can be a flat dollar figure, but for wind, hail, and named storms many policies use a percentage of the building's insured value instead, which can be a much larger number than people expect. Know which kind you have.

Your sublimits. A policy can carry a healthy overall limit while quietly capping specific things far lower. Flood and water backup, debris removal, certain types of equipment, and other categories can each have their own ceiling buried in the forms. A sublimit is the most you'll get for that category no matter how big your total limit looks.

Your extra expense and business income terms, if you have them. These are what fund the trailers, the temporary lease, the generator rental that keeps you operating while the building gets rebuilt. Knowing what's there before the loss tells you how fast you can move.

If you can't explain your wind deductible and your top three sublimits in plain language right now, that's the assignment for this week.

## 4. A documented contact tree

In the first hour after a loss, confusion costs you. People stand around assuming someone else made the call. Three different staff give three different stories to a reporter. Nobody starts the photos.

Write down, on one page, who does what:

- Who calls the broker and the carrier, and who is the backup if that person is unreachable.
- Who documents the damage with photos and video before anything gets touched.
- Who handles arranging emergency mitigation, the board-up, the water extraction, the tarp.
- Who is the single voice to families and to media. One voice. Everyone else routes to them.
- Who pulls the off-site policy folder and inventory.

Put names and cell numbers on it. Keep a copy off-site with the policies. A loss is going to happen on a Saturday or at 2 a.m., not during a calm staff meeting, and the plan only helps if it exists before the phone rings.

## 5. Mitigate immediately, because the policy requires it

Here is a piece of the contract most school leaders have never been told. Your property policy obligates you to take reasonable steps to protect the property from further damage after a loss. It's a standard duty, and ignoring it can reduce or jeopardize what the carrier pays.

In plain terms: if a pipe bursts, you're expected to shut the water and get the standing water out, not let it sit for a week and then bill the carrier for the mold that grew because you waited. Tarp the open roof. Board the broken window. Move the salvageable equipment to dry ground.

Two things make this work. First, keep the receipts, reasonable emergency mitigation is itself usually a covered cost. Second, and this is the part people miss, document the damage before you start cleaning it up. Mitigate fast, but photograph first.

## 6. Document everything, during and after

The cleanup instinct is strong and it works against you. Once the wreckage is hauled to a dumpster, your proof goes with it.

Photograph and video the damage from every angle before anyone moves anything. Keep damaged property where it is until the adjuster has seen it or has told you in writing it's fine to discard. Start a simple loss log the day it happens: a running record of what occurred, who you spoke with, what they said, and what you spent. Keep every receipt, the mitigation crew, the temporary equipment, the extra staff hours, all of it.

That log is boring to keep and decisive to have. Months later, when the details have gone fuzzy and the claim is in negotiation, the school with the contemporaneous record is the one whose version holds up.

## 7. Understand the timeline and the proof of loss

A property claim runs on a clock, and the clock has your name on it, not the carrier's.

Most policies require prompt notice of a loss, so report it quickly even before you know the full scope. Many then require a sworn proof of loss, a formal document where you state the amount you're claiming, typically due within a set window after the carrier requests it. There are deadlines for submitting your documentation and, often, time limits on how long you have to sue if it comes to that. Missing these isn't a paperwork hiccup. It can be the carrier's clean reason to deny.

You don't have to memorize the windows. You have to know they exist and have someone watching them, which is exactly what the next section is about.

## Where the generalist broker leaves you standing alone

Most of what's above never gets mentioned to a school because the broker who sold the policy treats the account like a renewal to process, not a client to protect. They emailed the dec page once, two years ago, buried in a PDF nobody opened. They never explained the wind deductible or the flood sublimit, and they certainly never walked the building to talk about an inventory.

Then the loss happens, and the difference shows. The generalist is hard to reach, slow to call back, and absent from the room where the adjuster decides what your damage is worth. They didn't prepare you before, and they don't advocate for you during. You're negotiating a six-figure claim alone, against a professional who does this every day, with records you're rebuilding from memory.

A broker who actually does the job builds this blueprint with you on a calm Tuesday. They make sure you understand your deductibles and sublimits before a storm tests them. And when the loss comes, they show up, push back on a lowball adjustment, and keep the timeline and the proof of loss from quietly running out on you. That presence at claim time is the whole reason a broker exists. It's also the moment the cheap, absent ones vanish.

## The question worth sitting with

Picture the worst Tuesday. A fire, a flood, a storm that opens the roof. Tonight.

Could you, tomorrow morning, hand someone a current inventory of what the building held, a copy of your policy from outside the building, and a one-page plan of who does what? Or would the honest answer be that you'd be starting all of it from scratch, in the wreckage, on the worst day your school has had?

Because that gap, between what you could prove today and what you'd wish you'd done, is the exact gap your carrier will price the claim inside of. Better to close it on a quiet afternoon than discover it the morning after.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a second set of eyes on your property coverage and a real disaster blueprint built before you ever need it, that's the kind of work we'd rather do now than during the cleanup.*

# The Six-Figure Mold Bill Your Property Policy Was Never Going to Pay

*A plain-English look at the pollution exclusion: the standard clause that quietly carves environmental problems out of the policies schools assume cover the building.*

---

A slow leak runs behind a wall for three months. By the time anyone smells it, mold has spread through two classrooms and a hallway. The remediation quote comes back at low six figures. Then two families and a teacher report breathing problems and lawyer up.

You file the claim on your property policy, because that is the policy that covers the building.

The adjuster points to one paragraph and denies it.

---

## The clause you were never walked through

You run a school in a building. Maybe it was built decades ago. Maybe it used to be a church, a warehouse, or a strip-mall storefront before it became a campus. You bought property insurance and general liability, you renew them every year, and you assume that between the two of them, the building and the people in it are covered for whatever the building throws at you.

For fire, wind, theft, a slip and fall, that assumption holds.

For environmental problems, it usually does not. Buried in nearly every standard commercial property policy and nearly every general liability policy is a section called the pollution exclusion. It does exactly what the name says. It removes pollution-related losses from coverage. And the definition of "pollutant" in these forms is broad enough to sweep in the everyday environmental issues that older and repurposed school buildings produce on their own.

So let me walk through it. What the exclusion is, why your building is exposed, and what actually responds when one of these claims lands.

## What the pollution exclusion actually does

Standard insurance forms generally define a pollutant as any solid, liquid, gaseous, or thermal irritant or contaminant. That includes smoke, vapor, fumes, chemicals, and waste. Courts and carriers have applied that language to a long list of substances that a school leader would never file mentally under "pollution."

Mold from a water leak. Lead, whether from old paint or old plumbing. Asbestos fibers released into the air. Fuel or heating oil that leaks from a storage tank. Sewage that backs up into a building. To you, those are maintenance problems and building problems. Under the policy language, they tend to land on the pollutant side of the line.

Here is how the gap opens. Your property policy is first-party coverage, meaning it pays to fix your own building. Your general liability policy is third-party coverage, meaning it pays when someone else gets hurt or their property gets damaged and they make a claim against you. The pollution exclusion sits in both. So when an environmental loss hits, the policy that would have paid to clean it up says no, and the policy that would have defended you against the injured family says no too. One event, two denials, and the bill stays with the school.

That is not a carrier being difficult. That is the policy working exactly as written. The exclusion has been standard for decades. The problem is that almost no one buying the policy gets told it is there or what it quietly removes.

## Why a charter school's building is the worst-case profile

Most charter schools are not operating out of purpose-built, brand-new campuses. They are in older stock, or in buildings converted from something else, because that is what is affordable and available. That single fact stacks the environmental exposure higher than almost any other tenant in town.

Older buildings come with older materials. Lead paint was common in construction for a long time, and lead service lines and lead solder show up in old plumbing, which puts it in the drinking water children use all day. Asbestos was used in floor tile, insulation, ceiling materials, and pipe wrap for decades, and it sits there harmlessly until a renovation disturbs it and sends fibers into the air. A repurposed industrial or commercial building can carry an old fuel oil tank, in the ground or the basement, that nobody on staff knows about.

Then layer the way a school uses a building. You pack hundreds of children, whose bodies are more vulnerable to these exposures than adults, into the space for six or seven hours a day. You renovate to add classrooms, which is exactly the activity that disturbs asbestos and lead. A slow plumbing leak feeds mold growth behind walls for weeks before anyone notices. An aging HVAC system produces indoor air quality complaints that turn into health claims.

Old materials, vulnerable occupants, constant occupancy, and ongoing renovation. That combination is the profile environmental coverage exists for, and it describes the typical charter school almost exactly.

## How the generalist broker leaves you holding it

When a broker sells a school a property policy and a general liability policy and stops there, they have sold you the building coverage anyone would expect. What they often never raise is the hole the pollution exclusion punches through both.

Some of it is laziness. Renewing the same two policies year after year is the easy button, and the broker who treats a charter school as a small account to clear off the desk is not reading the exclusions out loud to you. Some of it is that the broker never looked at your building. They never asked how old it is, whether it was converted from something else, whether there is an oil tank somewhere, whether you are about to renovate. So they never connected your specific building to the exposure their own forms exclude.

Either way, the result is the same. You found out the pollution exclusion existed at the exact moment the adjuster cited it, the worst possible time to learn what you do not own.

## What dedicated environmental coverage responds to

The fix is a separate policy built for this exact gap. It is usually called Pollution Legal Liability or a site pollution policy, and depending on the carrier and your situation, the protection can sometimes be added by endorsement rather than as a standalone form. The mechanics are what matter, so here is what this coverage is designed to do.

It responds on two fronts, the two fronts your standard policies exclude.

First-party cleanup. When mold, lead, asbestos, a fuel release, or a sewage event contaminates your own building, this coverage is built to pay the remediation and cleanup costs. That is the six-figure mold or lead-abatement bill your property policy hands back to you.

Third-party bodily injury and property damage. When students, staff, or families claim they were harmed by exposure to that contamination, or a neighboring property claims your contamination spread to them, this coverage is built to defend you and pay covered claims. That is the lawsuit your general liability policy steps away from the moment the word "pollutant" enters the file.

Coverage terms vary by carrier and by building, and a real environmental policy gets shaped around what your specific site presents. One form does not fix everything automatically. But there is a category of insurance designed precisely for the losses your two main policies carve out, and most schools have never been shown it exists.

## What it looks like when someone is doing the job

A broker who knows charter schools starts with the building, not the form. They ask how old it is and what it used to be. They ask whether you are planning a renovation, because disturbing old materials is the trigger event. They ask whether anyone has ever located, or even looked for, a fuel oil tank on the property.

Then they read your property and general liability policies and show you the pollution exclusion in plain language, so you see exactly what is carved out before a claim does it for you. And they price out dedicated environmental coverage scaled to your actual building, so the decision to carry it or skip it is one you make on purpose, with the numbers in front of you, instead of one that gets made for you by an exclusion you never read.

That is the difference between a broker selling two policies and a broker covering a school.

## The question worth sitting with

Your building is doing exactly what older buildings do, right now, whether anyone is watching for it or not. A pipe is aging behind a wall. Paint from another era is on a surface a child can reach. Something may be buried in the ground that predates everyone on your staff.

So here is the question to sit with before your next renewal.

If a slow leak turned into a mold remediation bill next month, and a family said their child got sick from it, do you actually know whether either of those lands inside your coverage or straight into the pollution exclusion? Or is the honest answer that nobody has ever pulled your policies and checked the one paragraph that decides it?

If you cannot answer that with confidence, you do not have a coverage problem yet. You have a paragraph nobody has read, sitting between your school and a six-figure bill.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a straight read on whether your building's environmental exposures are covered or excluded before a leak forces the question, that is a conversation we are glad to have.*

topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at Extra Expense and Business Income coverage: the two parts of your property policy that decide whether a building loss is a hard year or the end of the school.

hub.content_markdown:
# The Fire Doesn't Close Your School. The Eight Months Without a Classroom Does.

*A plain-English look at Extra Expense and Business Income coverage: the two parts of your property policy that decide whether a building loss is a hard year or the end of the school.*

---

A pipe bursts over a holiday weekend, or a fire takes the gym wing, or a storm peels the roof back and the rain ruins everything under it.

The building is the obvious loss. Your property insurance writes a check to rebuild it, and that part works mostly the way you'd expect.

Here's what nobody put in front of you. The rebuild takes seven, eight, maybe twelve months. Your school does not get to pause for twelve months. The kids still need a room, the staff still need paychecks, and your funding still depends on those kids showing up and being counted. The check that rebuilds the wall does nothing about any of that.

That gap, the cost of staying open while you can't use your building, is what this paper is about.

---

## Two different coverages doing two different jobs

People lump these together and then carry the wrong one. They are separate, and a school needs both.

**Business Income** replaces the money you stop bringing in while operations are interrupted. If a covered loss shuts down part or all of your school and your revenue drops, Business Income is meant to fill that hole, plus keep covering the expenses that don't stop just because the building did. Payroll, your loan or lease payments, utilities you're still on the hook for.

**Extra Expense** pays the added costs you take on specifically to keep operating, or to get back operating faster, after that same loss. Renting alternate space. Bringing in temporary or portable classrooms. The moving costs. Setting up phones, internet, servers, and student devices in the new spot. Busing students to wherever you've relocated them. None of these are costs you'd normally have. You're spending the money precisely so the school doesn't go dark.

One backfills lost revenue. The other funds the scramble to keep running. A serious property loss triggers both at once, and they work as a pair.

## Why a generic school misses this and a charter school can't afford to

For an ordinary business, Business Income is the headline and Extra Expense is the smaller sidekick. Customers go elsewhere for a few months and the revenue line takes the hit. So most commercial policies are built and sold with that shape in mind, Business Income front and center, Extra Expense as a modest add-on.

A charter school does not work like an ordinary business, and this is where the standard package quietly fails it.

Your revenue is per-pupil funding. It follows enrolled students who are actually attending and being counted. That changes the whole calculation, because you have a problem a coffee shop never faces: if you can't give families a classroom, those families don't wait. They enroll their kids somewhere else, and the funding that student carried leaves with them. It does not automatically come back when your building reopens.

So the real threat of a long closure isn't only the rebuild bill. Every week without a room is a week families are deciding whether to stay. Lose enough of them and you don't just lose this year's funding. You lose next year's enrollment and the financial footing the whole school stands on.

That flips the priority. For you, Extra Expense, the money that gets students back into a room somewhere fast, is often the coverage that saves the institution, because keeping students enrolled is the same thing as protecting your funding.

## The clock that controls all of it: the restoration period

Both coverages run on a timer, and hardly anyone gets shown how that timer is set.

The **restoration period** (sometimes called the period of restoration or indemnity period) is the window the insurer will pay for. It generally starts at the date of the loss and runs until the property is repaired or replaced, or should reasonably have been, with normal operations restored. Once that period closes, the payments stop, even if your school hasn't fully recovered.

Two things about that window catch schools off guard.

First, it tracks how long it should reasonably take to restore the property, not necessarily how long it actually drags out. Permitting delays, a contractor backlog, custom work, scarce materials: those can push your real timeline well past what the policy treats as reasonable. If your coverage assumed an eight-month restoration and the rebuild honestly takes fourteen, those last six months can land on you.

Second, on many policies the standard restoration period is shorter than what a full school rebuild requires. Between insurance adjusting, design, permits, construction, and inspection, large losses routinely run past a year. If the policy's clock runs out before your doors are truly back open, the coverage stops carrying you at the worst possible point.

There's an extension worth knowing by name. **Extended Business Income** keeps coverage running for a defined stretch after you reopen, while you're rebuilding enrollment back to where it was. For a school clawing students back after a long displacement, that tail can matter as much as the main coverage.

## Where the limit gets set too low, or skipped entirely

Two failures show up over and over on school policies, and both trace back to nobody modeling the actual scenario.

**Extra Expense is missing or token.** The policy was sold around Business Income, and Extra Expense came along as a small flat limit or got left off. Then a loss hits, the school needs portable classrooms and a leased annex and busing on day thirty, and the limit covers a fraction of it. The coverage built for the exact emergency you're in turns out to be the thinnest line on the page.

**The Business Income limit was a guess.** Someone picked a round number years ago, it renewed on autopilot every year since, and nobody ever tied it to your real budget, your real payroll, or how long you'd actually be down. A school running on per-pupil funding with months of payroll and fixed costs to cover can burn through a casually chosen limit fast.

The fix is not exotic. It's a number you build instead of guess.

## Best practice: model the displacement before it happens

Stop thinking about the limit as a number on a form and start thinking about a question. If we lost our main building tomorrow, how would we keep students in a room somewhere, how long would it realistically take, and what would every step cost?

Walk it through.

- **How long until students are back in a room somewhere?** Not the full rebuild. The interim. How fast could you stand up portable classrooms or lease nearby space, and what's the genuine lead time on each?
- **What does the interim cost, month by month?** Rent on alternate space, the portables, moving, rewiring IT and phones and student devices, busing students to the temporary site. Add it up across the months you'd realistically be displaced.
- **What revenue is exposed, and for how long?** Tie the Business Income limit to your real budget and per-pupil funding, and stress it against a restoration period long enough for a serious rebuild, not a hopeful one.
- **What happens to enrollment, and how long to win it back?** This is where Extended Business Income earns its place, covering the stretch after you reopen while families come back.

Run that exercise and the right limits stop being a guess. You set Extra Expense to fund a real relocation, Business Income to your actual exposure, and a restoration period that matches how long a school rebuild honestly takes. The exact figures depend on your budget and building, so they get built with you, not pulled off a shelf.

## What a broker who knows charter schools actually does

A generalist sells you the property policy, points at the building limit, and renews it every year without ever asking the one question that matters: how would this school keep operating if it couldn't use this building. So Extra Expense stays thin, the Business Income limit stays a guess, and the restoration period stays whatever the carrier defaulted to.

A broker who works with schools starts from the displacement. They map how you'd relocate students and how long it'd take, size Extra Expense and Business Income to that reality, check the restoration period against a real rebuild timeline, and make sure something like Extended Business Income is there for the enrollment you're rebuilding after you reopen. Then they show you the math in plain language instead of a flat limit you're told to trust.

The point isn't to make you fear a fire. It's to make sure that if one comes, the loss is a building, not the school.

## The question worth sitting with

Picture your main building unusable tomorrow morning. Families are calling, staff are waiting, and the funding clock is running on every student who can't find their classroom.

Do you know, with any confidence, how many months your current policy would actually keep this school operating and how much it would pay to get students back in a room somewhere? Or has that limit just renewed itself for years while everyone assumed the building check was the whole plan?

If you can't answer that, you don't have a coverage problem yet. You have a number nobody has ever checked against the day it would have to work.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want someone to model what it would actually take to keep your school running after a building loss, and set your Extra Expense and Business Income limits to match, that's the kind of work we'd rather do before the loss than after.*

topics.title: You Lent the Gym for a Saturday. Now Their Player's Lawyer Is Calling Your Insurer.
topics.subtitle: A youth league used your space for a weekend tournament. A kid got hurt. The claim landed on your school's liability policy, not theirs. Here is the paperwork that decides who actually pays.

hub.content_markdown:
# You Lent the Gym for a Saturday. Now Their Player's Lawyer Is Calling Your Insurer.

*A youth league used your space for a weekend tournament. A kid got hurt. The claim landed on your school's liability policy, not theirs. Here is the paperwork that decides who actually pays.*

---

A travel basketball league rents your gym for a Saturday tournament. Forty kids, a couple hundred parents in the bleachers, refs you have never met running the games.

In the third game, a player goes down hard chasing a loose ball, hits the wall padding wrong, and breaks a wrist that does not heal right.

The family hires a lawyer. The lawyer sues everyone with a name on the building, and that includes your school.

The league had its own insurance. You assumed that meant the league's insurance would handle it. So why is your carrier the one opening a claim file?

---

## The thing nobody walks you through

Your building rarely sits empty. A church holds Sunday services in your auditorium. A youth soccer club runs clinics on your field. A summer camp takes over the place in July. A vendor sets up a fall festival in your parking lot. Maybe your own team practices at the city rec center while your gym is being refinished.

Every one of those is a risk transfer waiting to happen, or one that quietly failed. The difference is almost entirely paperwork, and the paperwork is the part a generalist broker almost never raises with a school.

Here is what happens when an outside group uses your space and something goes wrong. An injured person, or their attorney, names every party they can find. The school owns the building, so the school gets named. From that moment, the only question that matters is whose insurance defends and pays. Do the paperwork right and the answer is the user's insurer. Skip it and the answer is yours.

Let me walk through how to make sure it is theirs.

## Risk transfer, in plain English

When you let an outside group use your facility, you want the risk of their activity to ride home with them, not stay parked on your policy. That is the entire goal. Four pieces make it work, and they only work together.

**A written facility use agreement.** The contract that says who is using the space, when, for what, and on what terms. It is where the rest of the protections live. A handshake and a key is not a facility use agreement.

**A current certificate of insurance from the user.** The certificate, often called a COI, is proof that the outside group carries its own general liability insurance, with the limits and dates shown. You want this in hand before they set foot in the building.

**Additional insured status naming your school on the user's policy.** The piece that does the heavy lifting, and the one most people think they already have and do not. More on this in a second, because the distinction is where schools get hurt.

**A hold-harmless and indemnification clause inside the agreement.** The contractual promise that if their activity causes a loss, they cover the school for it, including defense costs. It is the legal backbone behind the insurance pieces.

Skip any one and the structure leaks. Most schools have one or two, usually the wrong ones.

## A certificate of insurance is not the same as being additional insured

This is the misunderstanding that costs schools the most, so read it twice.

A certificate of insurance proves the outside group has a policy. That is all it does. It is a snapshot, a piece of evidence. It does not give your school any rights under their policy. If you are only holding a COI when a claim hits, you are a bystander looking at proof that someone else has coverage you cannot actually use.

Additional insured status is a different animal. When the user's policy is endorsed to name your school as an additional insured, your school becomes a protected party on their policy for claims arising out of their use of your space. Their insurer steps in to defend and pay first, on their dime, for a loss tied to their activity. Their policy, their limits, their lawyers, ahead of yours.

The actual additional insured status comes from an endorsement on the user's policy. The certificate may reference it, but the certificate is not the endorsement. A COI that simply lists your school in the description box, with no endorsement behind it, can leave you holding nothing when it counts. When you can, ask to see the endorsement itself, not just the certificate that claims it exists.

Put simply: the certificate tells you they have insurance. The endorsement is what puts that insurance to work for you.

## Why this lands harder on schools

A business that lends out a conference room takes on some risk. A school takes on more, for two reasons.

First, the activities are higher-energy and involve kids. Sports, camps, performances, festivals. More motion, more people, more ways for someone to get hurt on your floor.

Second, schools say yes a lot. Being a community hub is part of the mission. The church needs a space, the league needs a court, the scouts need a room, and a school leader who cares wants to help. That generosity is good. Without the paperwork behind it, it means your liability policy is quietly subsidizing every outside group that walks through the door.

When the loss comes, it does not sort itself by whose fault it was. It chases the building, the deep pocket, the named insured on file. That is you, until the paperwork says otherwise.

## How the gap got there

Walk into most schools and ask who reviews the insurance on the groups using the building. The honest answer is usually nobody. A front-office staffer hands over a key, the facilities calendar fills in, and the insurance question never comes up.

That is not really the school's failure. It is the broker's. A broker who knew charter schools would have asked, years ago, a simple question: who uses your building when students are not in it? Then they would have handed you a facility use process and a checklist so the answer was handled every time, not improvised by whoever was at the front desk.

The generalist does not ask, because asking means understanding how your building actually gets used, and that is more work than processing a renewal. So the renewal gets processed and the gym keeps getting lent out on a handshake. The gap is not bad luck. It is the predictable result of an account nobody is minding.

## What to require before you hand over the keys

This is the part you can act on. Treat it as a standing checklist that every outside use clears before access is granted.

1. **A signed facility use agreement** for the specific dates and activity. No agreement, no access.
2. **A current certificate of insurance** showing general liability, with limits your school sets in advance, and dates that actually cover the use. An expired COI is the same as no COI.
3. **Additional insured status** naming your school on the user's policy, confirmed by endorsement, not just a line typed into the certificate. For sports leagues and camps especially, this is non-negotiable.
4. **A hold-harmless and indemnification clause** in the agreement, written so the user defends and indemnifies the school for losses arising from their use.
5. **The right scope and limits for the activity.** A chess club is not a tackle football camp. Match what you require to the risk in front of you.
6. **A named, responsible contact** for the using organization, on the agreement, who is accountable for their people.
7. **A file that someone actually keeps.** Agreement, COI, and endorsement stored together, refreshed when a group returns next season. Coverage that lapsed in March does you no good in October.

Run a group through that list before they get a key, and the risk of their activity rides home on their policy where it belongs. Skip it, and you find out what you were missing on the worst day, in a deposition.

## The flip side: when your school is the guest

It runs the other direction too. When your team practices at the city rec center, or your students perform in a rented hall, or you hold an offsite event in someone else's building, expect that organization to ask you for the exact same things. A use agreement. A certificate of insurance. Additional insured status naming them on your policy. An indemnification clause.

That is not them being difficult. That is them doing precisely what you should be doing. A broker who knows schools makes issuing those certificates and endorsements quick and routine, so your programs never get stalled at someone else's front desk because your paperwork was not ready.

## The question worth sitting with

Think about the last outside group that used your building. The league, the church, the camp, the vendor in the parking lot.

Did your school sign an agreement with them, hold a current certificate, and get named as an additional insured on their policy by endorsement? Or did someone just hand over a key because they seemed legitimate and the calendar said the space was open?

If you cannot answer that with confidence for the groups in your building right now, you do not have a coverage problem yet. You have a question nobody has checked. The check is cheap. The Saturday it goes wrong without it is not.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a second set of eyes on who is using your building and whether the paperwork actually transfers the risk, that is the kind of thing we do before anyone gets hurt.*

topics.title: The Six-Figure Loss That Happens While Your Building Is Empty
topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at water damage, the most common and most preventable property loss at schools, and the cheap connected sensors that catch it before it becomes a claim.

hub.content_markdown:
# The Six-Figure Loss That Happens While Your Building Is Empty

*A plain-English look at water damage, the most common and most preventable property loss at schools, and the cheap connected sensors that catch it before it becomes a claim.*

---

It's the Tuesday after a long weekend. A custodian opens the building and the first floor is under an inch of water.

A supply line behind a second-floor utility sink let go on Friday night. It ran for roughly sixty hours into an empty building. Drywall, flooring, two classrooms of materials, the server closet below. Now you're looking at remediation crews, mold testing, a displaced wing, and a number with a comma in the wrong place.

Nobody did anything wrong. That's what makes it sting. The building was locked, the doors were checked, the alarm was set. The water just had a clear weekend to do its work, and no one was there to hear it.

---

## The loss nobody plans for, because it isn't dramatic

When school leaders think about property risk, they picture fire or a storm. Those are real. They are also relatively rare, and they tend to announce themselves loudly.

Water doesn't announce anything. It is the quiet, steady, expensive one. Across school property claims, water damage is consistently among the most frequent and most costly causes of loss, and the most maddening part is how preventable most of it is. The usual suspects are boring on purpose:

- A burst or cracked pipe, especially during a cold snap
- A failed water heater that lets go all at once
- An HVAC condensate line or coil that leaks for days
- A roof leak that finds its way into a ceiling and keeps going
- A toilet supply line or valve that fails over a break

Notice the pattern in the opening story, because it runs through almost all of these. The damage rarely happens while the building is full of people who would hear the drip, smell the damp, or step in the puddle. It happens overnight, over a weekend, over winter break, over the summer. The leak itself might be small. The clock is what turns a small leak into a gut renovation.

## Why time is the whole problem

A pipe that fails on a Friday at 6 p.m. in an occupied building is a mop and a phone call. The same pipe failing on a Friday at 6 p.m. in a building no one will enter until Monday is a catastrophe. Same defect, same water pressure. The only variable that changed is how long it ran before someone shut it off.

That single variable, time-to-discovery, is where the entire loss lives. It is also the one thing technology can now collapse to almost nothing, for a price that would embarrass anyone who's seen a real water claim.

## What the sensors actually do, in plain English

You don't need a smart building or an IT department for this. The category is cheap, mature, and boring in the best way. Three types matter for a school.

**Leak and water sensors with automatic shutoff.** A small puck-style sensor sits where water shouldn't be: under sinks, behind water heaters, near the boiler, at the base of HVAC units, in the server closet. The moment it senses water, it alerts. The version worth paying for adds an automatic shutoff valve on the main or on a zone. When the system detects a leak or an abnormal continuous flow, it closes the valve and stops the water on its own, without waiting for a human to drive in at 2 a.m. That is the feature that turns sixty hours of flooding into sixty seconds.

**Temperature and freeze sensors.** Cheap probes that watch for the building dropping toward freezing in a mechanical room, an attic, an exterior wall cavity, anywhere a pipe could freeze and split. A freeze alert at 28 degrees on a Saturday is a heads-up. A frozen pipe discovered Monday is a claim.

**Humidity sensors.** These catch the slow leak, the one with no dramatic burst. Rising humidity in a normally dry space is often the first sign of a roof leak, a hidden pipe weep, or HVAC trouble, long before water ever pools where you'd see it.

None of this is exotic and none of it requires ripping open walls. Most of it is battery-powered, sticks in place, and talks to a hub over Wi-Fi or cellular.

## A sensor that pings an empty office is just a noise

Here is the part people skip, and it's the part that actually matters. Detection without response is theater. A sensor that fires an alert into an inbox nobody reads over the weekend has prevented nothing.

The sensors are only as good as what happens in the ninety seconds after they trip. So the device is half the system. The other half is the plan:

- Alerts go to multiple people, not one. A phone, a text, an email, ideally to the facilities lead and a backup who is actually reachable on a Sunday.
- Someone knows what to do with the alert. Where the shutoff is, who to call, when to escalate.
- The highest-stakes locations get the automatic shutoff valve, so the response doesn't depend on a human being awake and nearby.

Decide that before you buy a single puck. The technology buys you time. A response plan is what spends that time well.

## Where to put them

You don't sensor the whole building. You sensor the places where water lives and the places where a leak would be most expensive. Start with the mechanical room and the water heater, because that's where the big-volume failures originate. Then the server and electrical closets, because that's where a small amount of water does outsized damage. Then under every sink and near every restroom supply line, plus the base of interior HVAC air handlers. Freeze sensors go in unheated or poorly heated spaces with pipe runs. Humidity sensors go under the roof and anywhere a slow leak would hide. It's a short walk with a facilities person and a notepad, not an engineering study.

## The carrier wants this more than you'd guess

Here's where it gets genuinely useful, and where most schools have no idea what they're leaving on the table.

Your insurance carrier does not want to pay your water claim. A six-figure water loss is a bad day for them too. Preventing the claim is dramatically cheaper than paying it, which is why a growing number of carriers actively encourage this kind of mitigation. Depending on the carrier and the program, that support can take several forms. Some offer premium credits for installing monitored leak detection or automatic shutoff. Some provide the devices free or subsidized through a partner. Some include risk-engineering or loss-control help, a real human who will walk your building and tell you where the exposures are.

The specifics vary a lot by carrier, and I'm not going to quote you a percentage or name a program, because the honest answer is that it depends on who writes your coverage and what they currently offer. What stays true across all of them is the direction of the incentive. The carrier would rather spend a little to prevent the loss than a lot to indemnify it. That makes risk prevention a partnership your carrier wants to have with you. Most schools never find out, because nobody ever brought it up.

## Why nobody brought it up

A generalist broker treats insurance as a purely reactive product. You have a loss, you file a claim, they process it, renewal comes around, repeat. Mitigation programs, carrier loss-control resources, available credits for sensors: none of that shows up in an easy-button renewal, because surfacing it would mean reading the carrier's current offerings and actually talking to you about your building.

So it doesn't come up. Not because the broker is hiding it. Because finding it and explaining it is work, and the account got priced as too small to bother with. The credit sits there unused. The loss-control visit never gets scheduled. The school keeps paying full freight for coverage while leaving the prevention dollars on the table, and then one Tuesday the first floor is under an inch of water and everyone acts surprised.

A broker doing the job asks a different question at renewal. What does your carrier offer for risk mitigation right now, what would qualify your school for a credit, and would they send someone to walk the building. Then they help you stand up the sensors and the response plan so the coverage is backed by prevention, not just waiting on a claim.

## The question worth sitting with

Walk your building in your head for a second. The mechanical room, the water heater, the closet under the server, the restrooms at the far end of an empty wing. Picture a line letting go on a Friday night over a long weekend.

How long would it run before anyone knew? And has anyone, your current broker included, ever told you what your carrier would do to help you stop that from happening, or even asked to walk the building? Because the cheapest claim you will ever have is the one that never happens, and right now you may be paying for protection while leaving the prevention sitting in a box nobody opened.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want to know what mitigation credits and loss-control support your carrier actually offers, and where the cheap sensors should go in your building, that's a short conversation worth having before the next long weekend.*

hub.content_markdown:
# Can a Teacher Drive Students to the Game? Can a Parent? Here Is the Straight Answer Most Schools Never Get.

*Field trips, athletic events, and the carpool to the science museum raise the same questions every single year. Who is allowed to drive, who is covered when something happens, and how do you run it the right way.*

---

Every year, like clockwork, a school leader asks me some version of the same question.

Can our teachers drive students to a game? A parent offered to take three kids to the field trip in her minivan, is that allowed? What if a parent wants to drive a whole carload of students across town? And the one underneath all of them: if there is a fender bender, who pays?

These are good questions. They come up constantly because charter schools run on this kind of everyday driving. The trip to the museum, the ride to the regional tournament, the supply run before the performance. Almost every school I have worked with has this exposure sitting in its week, whether anyone has named it or not.

The frustrating part is how often schools never get a clear answer. The topic lives in a grey area, so the safe-sounding response is a shrug or a "probably not, better not risk it." That leaves good programs afraid to do normal things.

So let me give you the straight version. Yes, staff and parents can drive students. Yes, that exposure can be insured, and I have confirmed it with carriers directly. And there is a right way to run it that protects the kids, the drivers, and the school all at once. Here is how it actually works.

## The coverage that handles this, in plain English

The protection this calls for is Hired and Non-Owned Auto liability, usually shortened to HNOA. The name describes it poorly, so let me translate.

"Non-owned auto" means a vehicle the school does not own but that gets used for the school's business. A teacher's sedan. A parent volunteer's minivan. A coach's truck hauling gear to a game. The school owns none of them, but the moment someone drives one on the school's behalf, the school can be drawn into the liability if something goes wrong.

"Hired auto" means a vehicle the school rents or borrows, like a passenger van rented for a trip.

HNOA covers the school's liability arising from that driving. When a staff member or parent is running the school's errand and an accident injures someone, HNOA is the coverage that responds on the school's behalf. It is a common, well-understood endorsement. This is not exotic, and it is not a favor a carrier is reluctant to do. It exists precisely because schools and nonprofits drive this way all the time.

## The part that surprises almost everyone

Here is the piece that clears up most of the confusion, and it is worth reading twice.

HNOA covers the school's *liability*. It does not repair the driver's own vehicle. If a teacher or a parent gets in a fender bender while driving students, the damage to their personal car is paid by their personal auto policy. Geico, Allstate, State Farm, whoever they carry. There is no version of the school's policy that pays to fix an employee's or a parent's personal vehicle. First-party physical damage on a personal car always lives with the owner's personal policy. Full stop.

Think of it as two separate lanes:

- **The driver's personal auto policy** handles their own car and is the first layer of liability when they cause an accident.
- **The school's HNOA** sits on top of that, protecting the school itself from the liability that flows uphill when the driver was doing the school's business.

The one nuance worth knowing: if the school rents a van for a trip, the school can choose to insure physical damage to that rented vehicle, because a hired vehicle is a different animal than someone's personal car. But a staff member's or parent's own vehicle is never the school's to repair.

## "The driver has their own insurance, so we are fine"

Their insurance is the foundation, and it is doing real work. It just is not the whole picture.

A personal auto policy is primary, so it pays first when the driver causes a wreck. But personal limits are often modest, and a serious-injury claim can blow through them quickly. The instant that personal policy is exhausted, an injured party's attorney looks for the next available source, and the school is standing right there, named because the driver was on the school's errand. HNOA is what stands behind the school at that point.

There is a second wrinkle. Some personal auto policies limit or restrict coverage when a vehicle is used for business or for a fee. A driver who has never thought about it can find their own insurer narrowing the claim. That is exactly why the documentation in the next section matters so much.

The takeaway is not "do not let people drive." It is "let people drive, and build the structure that makes it safe and covered."

## Best practices: how to run student driving the right way

This is where a school turns a grey area into a clean, defensible program. None of this is expensive. It is mostly process, and it is the difference between a confident yes and a nervous maybe.

**Confirm HNOA is actually on the policy, with real limits.** Adequate liability limits are what stand between the school and a serious claim. Verify it in writing. Do not assume it rode along automatically.

**Run motor vehicle records (MVRs) on regular drivers.** Anyone who routinely transports students should have their driving record checked. It is inexpensive and it screens out the worst risks before they ever get behind the wheel with kids. Re-run them on a set schedule, not just once.

**Collect and keep the declarations page from each driver's personal auto policy.** Confirm the policy is active and that the limits meet a minimum the school sets in advance. Keep the dec page on file and refresh it each year. This is your proof the primary layer exists, and it protects the driver as much as the school.

**Have each driver sign an acknowledgment.** A short form stating that they understand their personal auto policy is primary, that they accept responsibility for safe operation of their vehicle, and that they hold the school harmless for damage to their own car. Plain language, signed and dated.

**Get parent permission for the specific arrangement.** When a student rides with a particular driver, have the parent sign off acknowledging and approving it. If parents are driving other people's children, the same consent applies in both directions.

**Have drivers sign off on the rules of the road.** A one-page set of expectations: valid license required, seatbelts for every passenger, no phone use while driving, no unscheduled stops, no driving when impaired or exhausted, and a clear route. Simple rules, agreed to up front, prevent the worst outcomes.

**Keep an approved-driver roster.** Maintain a current list of who has cleared the MVR, submitted a dec page, and signed the acknowledgments. Before a trip, the organizer pulls from the roster instead of improvising in the parking lot.

That is the whole program. Coverage confirmed, records checked, dec pages on file, three short sign-offs, and a roster. A folder, basically, that turns an anxious question into a routine yes.

## What a broker who knows charter schools will do

A broker paying attention asks the question a generalist skips: who drives for your school in vehicles the school does not own, and across field trips, athletics, errands, and volunteers. Then they confirm HNOA is on the policy with limits that match the real activity, and they help you stand up the driver program above so the coverage is backed by good practice.

The point is not to scare a school out of field trips. It is to let the school do field trips, games, and museum runs with a clear answer and a clean process behind it.

## The question worth sitting with

Next month a parent is going to offer to drive a few students somewhere, or a coach is going to load up athletes for a game. When that happens, ask yourself this.

Do you have a clear yes ready, a simple process behind it, and coverage you have actually seen on your policy to back it up? Or is the honest answer "I would have to check"? Because that is a much better thing to know before the trip pulls out of the lot than after.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a straight answer on how your school can transport students safely and stay covered doing it, that is a short conversation we are glad to have before your next trip.*
