# Company Page Spoke: Hired and Non-Owned Auto Driver Program Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Can teachers and parents drive your students? Yes. Here is exactly who pays for what, and how to run it the right way.

Charter schools run on everyday driving: field trips, athletic events, the carpool to the museum, the supply run before a performance. Staff and parents driving students is allowed, and it is coverable. The questions schools actually need answered are who is covered and who pays which part. Here is the breakdown, then the program we put in place on every school we work with.

## Who pays for what

| Source | What it covers | What it does NOT cover |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver's personal auto policy** (Geico, Allstate, State Farm, etc.) | The driver's own vehicle (first-party physical damage) AND their primary liability when they cause an accident | Nothing about this layer covers the school's separate liability beyond the driver's limits |
| **School's Hired and Non-Owned Auto (HNOA)** | The school's third-party liability, sitting excess over the driver's personal liability, when a non-owned or hired vehicle is driven on school business | Repair of an employee's or a parent's personal car (that always stays with their personal policy) |
| **Hired-auto physical damage** (optional) | Physical damage to a vehicle the school rents, such as a passenger van for a trip | Personal vehicles; only applies to a rented or "hired" vehicle |

The driver's personal policy is primary. It pays first, and it is the only thing that ever repairs the driver's own car. The school's HNOA is the liability layer that protects the school when those personal limits are exhausted or when business use is questioned.

## The driver program checklist

- [ ] Confirm HNOA is on the policy with adequate liability limits, in writing. Do not assume it rode along automatically.
- [ ] Run motor vehicle records (MVRs) on anyone who regularly transports students, and re-run on a set schedule.
- [ ] Collect the declarations page from each driver's personal auto policy, confirm it is active and meets a minimum limit you set in advance, and keep it on file. Refresh annually.
- [ ] Have each driver sign an acknowledgment / hold-harmless: their personal auto policy is primary, and they hold the school harmless for damage to their own vehicle.
- [ ] Get parent consent for the specific driver and arrangement, in both directions when parents drive other families' children.
- [ ] Have drivers sign off on the rules of the road: valid license, seatbelts for every passenger, no phone use, no unscheduled stops, no impaired or exhausted driving, a clear route.
- [ ] Maintain an approved-driver roster of everyone who has cleared the MVR, submitted a dec page, and signed the forms. The trip organizer pulls from the roster.

## Why this is standard practice for us

Schools rarely get a clear answer here because the topic gets treated as a grey area, so the result is a shrug. We confirm HNOA is on the policy with limits that match the real activity, then help stand up the driver program above so the coverage is backed by good practice. The point is not to keep a school from running field trips. It is to let the school run them with a clear answer and a clean process behind it.

topics.title: You Are Probably Paying for a Toolbox You Have Never Opened
topics.subtitle: A plain-English audit of the free and low-cost services bundled into school insurance programs, the ones you fund inside your premium every year and almost never use.

hub.content_markdown:
# You Are Probably Paying for a Toolbox You Have Never Opened

*A plain-English audit of the free and low-cost services bundled into school insurance programs, the ones you fund inside your premium every year and almost never use.*

---

Two schools buy the same coverage from the same kind of program. Same premium, roughly.

One of them spent six hundred dollars last year on an outside HR consultant to review a termination. The other school called a hotline that came free with its policy, talked to an employment attorney for forty minutes, and paid nothing.

Same money in. One school used what it bought. The other paid for it and let it sit in a drawer.

---

## The stuff you funded and forgot

Insurance programs built for schools and nonprofits are rarely just a stack of coverage. The carriers and program administrators that specialize in this space know the accounts they write are short on staff and long on risk. So they bundle in services. Risk-management portals, employment-law hotlines, training libraries, screening discounts, sometimes a free site visit. These are real, they are useful, and a chunk of their cost is already baked into the premium you pay.

You funded them. The question is whether anyone ever showed you the door.

Most school leaders I talk to have no idea what is included with their program. Not because they are careless. Because nobody walked them through it. The policy got placed, the binder got emailed, and the value-adds went unmentioned. That is the gap this piece is about, and below is a practical way to close it.

A quick word of caution before the list. These services vary by program, by carrier, and by the size and type of your account. Nothing here is guaranteed to be on your policy. The point of this paper is not to tell you what you have. It is to tell you what to ask about, and then how to actually turn it on.

## What tends to be in the box

Here is the range of value-added services that commonly ride along with school and nonprofit insurance programs. Treat this as a menu to ask about, not a promise.

**Risk-management and safety resource portals.** Many programs include a web portal stocked with checklists, safety guides, and incident-tracking tools. Login credentials get issued once, usually to whoever signed the application, and then the link dies in an inbox.

**HR and employment-law hotlines.** A phone or email line, often staffed by employment attorneys or HR professionals, that you can call before you discipline, demote, or fire someone. For a school without in-house counsel, this is one of the most valuable things in the bundle and one of the least used.

**Background-screening discounts.** Programs sometimes negotiate reduced rates with screening vendors for staff and volunteer checks. If you are paying retail for background checks, you may be leaving a discount you already qualify for on the table.

**Online compliance and staff training libraries.** Courses on harassment prevention, mandated-reporter duties, bloodborne pathogens, FERPA basics, and similar topics. These often come with completion tracking, which is exactly the documentation you want when a regulator or a plaintiff's attorney asks what training you provided.

**Sample policy and handbook templates.** Vetted templates for employee handbooks, social media policies, acceptable-use agreements, and the like. Not legal advice, but a far better starting point than a document you copied off the internet in 2019.

**Loss-control and site visits.** Some programs offer a free or subsidized walkthrough of your facility by a risk professional who flags hazards before they become claims. Schools often think of this as something the carrier does to them. It is closer to a free consultation you are entitled to.

**Employee assistance programs (EAP).** Confidential counseling and referral services for staff dealing with stress, grief, substance issues, or financial strain. Frequently bundled on the benefits side. Frequently invisible to the employees who need it because nobody promoted it.

**Telemedicine.** Virtual access to a clinician, sometimes attached to a health plan and sometimes to a standalone program, that can keep staff out of urgent care for routine issues.

**Cyber-hygiene tools and breach-response hotlines.** With cyber coverage you may get phishing-simulation tools, security training, and most importantly a breach-response hotline. That hotline is the number you call in the first chaotic hour of a suspected breach, and calling it correctly can change how a claim plays out.

That is a long list. You are unlikely to have all of it. You almost certainly have more of it than you are using.

## Why none of this reached you

Step back and ask why a school can pay for these services for years and never touch them. The mechanics of the insurance are not the problem here. The mechanics of the relationship are.

A generalist broker treats a charter school like a small commodity account. The school is one file among hundreds, and the economics of that broker's book reward placing the policy and moving on. So the value-adds get sold exactly once, in the proposal, as bullet points that make the program look richer. Then they never get onboarded. Nobody activates the portal login. Nobody circulates the hotline number to the leadership team. Nobody schedules the loss-control visit. Nobody tells the staff the EAP exists.

The services were never the point of the sale. They were decoration on the quote. Once the ink dried, the broker had no reason to come back and make sure you used them, because using them does not pay the broker anything. Onboarding takes time, and time spent onboarding a small account is time a commodity broker would rather spend chasing a bigger one.

So the perks sit there, funded and dormant, until your annual renewal recycles the same unread bullet points into next year's proposal.

## The perks audit: what to ask, and how to turn it on

You can fix this in an afternoon. Here is the audit. Pull your current policies and your most recent proposal, then work the categories.

**Ask for the full list, in writing.** Tell your broker or carrier you want a written inventory of every value-added service included with each policy you hold. Property, liability, management liability, cyber, benefits, all of it. Make them produce the list rather than reciting it from memory.

**Match the list to credentials.** For every portal, training library, or online tool, get the actual login URL, the account credentials, and the name of who holds the master account. Reset it if the credentials trace back to a staffer who left two years ago.

**Find the hotline numbers and put them where they belong.** The HR and employment-law line, the breach-response line, the EAP line. Write them on a single sheet, give it to every person who might need to dial in a crisis, and decide in advance who is authorized to call. A hotline you cannot find at 9 p.m. on a Friday is not a benefit.

**Run the screening-discount math.** Ask what background-screening rates the program offers and compare them to what you pay now. If there is a gap, switch vendors or claim the rate.

**Schedule the loss-control visit on purpose.** If a site visit or risk assessment is included, request it rather than waiting to be offered it. Treat the findings as a free roadmap for reducing the claims that drive your future premium.

**Assign each live perk an owner.** A portal nobody owns is a portal nobody uses. Put a name next to each active service so there is one person responsible for actually working it during the year.

**Promote the staff-facing perks.** The EAP and telemedicine only return value if employees know they exist. Announce them at a staff meeting, put them in the handbook, repeat it at open enrollment.

**Re-audit at renewal.** Programs change what they include. Make this list a standing item every renewal, so a perk that quietly disappears or quietly appears does not go unnoticed for a year.

Work that list once and you will likely find services worth real money that you have been funding and ignoring.

## What it looks like when someone actually does the job

A broker who knows charter schools does not stop at placing the policy. They hand you the inventory of included services without being asked. They walk the leadership team through the hotlines and confirm the portal logins work. They tell your staff the EAP is there. They schedule the loss-control visit and sit with you on the findings. At renewal, they show you what changed in the bundle, not just what changed in the premium.

That is not a heroic effort. It is onboarding the thing you already bought. The reason it so rarely happens is that the broker on the account decided your school was too small to bother walking through the box.

## The question worth sitting with

Picture your most recent insurance proposal, the one with the bullet points listing everything the program "includes." You signed it. The premium reflects those bullets.

So ask yourself honestly. Of all the value-added services on that list, how many have you actually logged into, called, scheduled, or told your staff about in the past year? And if the honest answer is closer to none than to all, was that because the services had no value, or because nobody ever opened the box and showed you what you already paid for?

If you cannot name what is in your program, you do not have a coverage problem. You have a toolbox sitting unopened, and the only cost of opening it is the afternoon it takes to ask.

---

If you want a straight inventory of what your current program actually includes, and help turning the useful parts on, that is the kind of walk-through we do before anyone asks.*

topics.title: A Student Breaks a Wrist in PE and Nobody Did Anything Wrong. Guess Who the Family Sues Anyway.
topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at student accident insurance: the no-fault coverage that pays a hurt kid's medical bills even when the school is not at fault, and quietly heads off the lawsuit that starts when those bills go unpaid.

hub.content_markdown:
# A Student Breaks a Wrist in PE and Nobody Did Anything Wrong. Guess Who the Family Sues Anyway.

*A plain-English look at student accident insurance: the no-fault coverage that pays a hurt kid's medical bills even when the school is not at fault, and quietly heads off the lawsuit that starts when those bills go unpaid.*

---

A seventh grader lands wrong during a normal PE drill and snaps a wrist. No bad equipment. No negligent supervision. Just a kid who jumped and came down at a bad angle, the way kids have for as long as there have been gym floors.

The family files on their own health plan. It runs through a deductible, leaves them a few thousand dollars in bills, and then the questions start. Why are we paying for something that happened at your school. You were supposed to be watching. Somebody owes us for this.

Three weeks later a demand letter shows up with the school's name on it.

Nobody did anything wrong, and the school is now the defendant.

---

## Why your liability policy does not help here

This is the part most school leaders have backwards, and it is not their fault, because nobody walked them through it.

Your general liability policy is fault-based. It is third-party coverage, meaning it responds when someone outside the school claims the school caused them harm and is legally responsible for it. The trigger is negligence. If a family can show the school did something careless, a broken railing left for months, a coach who ignored a known hazard, inadequate supervision, then liability coverage steps in to defend the school and pay what the school legally owes.

Read that again, because the gap lives in one word. Negligence.

When a kid gets hurt and no one was careless, and that is the majority of school injuries, sports, playground, PE, the ordinary collisions of childhood, there is no negligence to trigger the policy. The liability carrier looks at the claim and says, correctly, that the school is not legally liable, so there is nothing to pay. The policy did its job. It just was never built to cover this.

So the medical bills land on the family. And a family staring at a bill they did not budget for, for an injury that happened on your watch, does not care about the legal definition of negligence. They care that their kid got hurt at your school and they are out of pocket. That anger is where lawsuits come from. Not always because the school was careless, but because a lawsuit is the only lever a frustrated family has left to get those bills covered.

## The coverage built for exactly this

Student accident insurance is a different animal. It is first-party, no-fault medical coverage. Strip the jargon and it means this: if a covered student gets hurt in a covered activity, the plan pays toward their medical expenses regardless of who was at fault. No one has to be blamed. No negligence has to be proven. A kid got hurt at school, the plan pays toward the bills, full stop.

Put the two side by side and the difference is clean.

- **General liability** is fault-based and third-party. It pays only if the school is found legally negligent, and it pays the injured party on the school's behalf.
- **Student accident** is no-fault and first-party. It pays toward the student's own medical bills because the injury happened, period, with no finding of fault required.

That no-fault piece is the whole point. It is what keeps a family whole after an injury that was genuinely nobody's fault. And a family whose bills got handled rarely goes looking for a lawyer. You can see how this works as risk management and not just as a kindness. The coverage that pays the bill on the front end is often the same coverage that prevents the liability claim on the back end. Handle the medical expense early, and the demand letter frequently never gets written.

## The structures, and how to match them to your school

Student accident coverage is not one product. It comes in a few common shapes, and the right setup depends on what your school actually does day to day.

**Blanket coverage for all students.** The school buys a plan that covers every enrolled student for injuries during school hours and school-sponsored activities. The school carries the cost, every kid is covered, and there is nothing for families to sign up for or remember. This is the cleanest base layer for a school that wants a real answer the moment any student gets hurt.

**Interscholastic athletics coverage.** Sports carry the heaviest injury exposure a school has, by a wide margin, and athletic plans are written specifically for that. This is also where the most important number on the page lives: the catastrophic injury limit. A torn ACL is expensive. A spinal cord injury, a serious head injury, the rare catastrophic event in contact sports, can run into the hundreds of thousands of dollars or more, over a lifetime of care. A plan with a high catastrophic limit is what stands between a school's athletic program and the kind of loss that ends programs and pulls a family into court out of sheer financial desperation. If your school fields competitive teams, especially in higher-contact sports, this is not optional.

**Voluntary plans offered to families.** Some plans are offered to parents to buy directly, often at low cost, covering their child for school-time injuries and sometimes around the clock. The school is not paying the premium, it is making a sensible option available. These work as a supplement, not a substitute for a blanket plan, and they are most useful for filling the gap families themselves carry.

**How it coordinates with the family's own health insurance.** This is the detail that decides how much the plan actually does, and it is the one a busy school leader is least likely to ask about. A student accident plan is either primary or excess.

A **primary** plan pays first, from the first dollar of covered expense, before the family's own health insurance is touched. An **excess** plan pays second, picking up costs after the family's health plan has paid its share, things like the deductible, the copays, the coinsurance left behind. Both are legitimate. They are built for different jobs and they cost differently. The mistake is not knowing which one you bought, then assuming the plan will catch a bill it was never structured to catch. An excess plan does nothing for a family that has no health insurance to go first. A school serving a community where uninsured or underinsured families are common needs to understand that distinction before an injury, not after.

## Why the gap is sitting open in most schools

Here is the honest reason most charter schools do not have this coverage, or have the wrong version of it. A generalist broker placed the school's package, checked the box on general liability, and never raised student accident at all. It was not on the renewal. It was not in the conversation. The easy-button renewal rolls the same package forward every year, premium roughly flat, nobody reads the policy, and the absence of a coverage no one mentioned is invisible until a kid gets hurt and a family asks who is paying.

A broker who actually works with schools knows that student injuries are not a rare event, they are a Tuesday. They know the liability policy will not answer for the no-fault ones. And they know that an unpaid medical bill is the seed most school injury lawsuits grow from. Raising student accident coverage is not upselling. It is doing the part of the job that a school assumed was already being done.

## What it looks like when someone is actually paying attention

A broker who knows charter schools maps the coverage to the activities. They look at what the school runs, the athletic program and its contact level, the playground, the PE curriculum, the field trips, and they match the structure to it. They tell you in plain language whether the plan is primary or excess, and whether that fits the families you serve. They make sure the catastrophic limit on the athletics side is real and not decorative. And they frame the whole thing the way it should be framed, as the coverage that keeps families whole and keeps lawsuits from starting, not as a line item to shave off the quote.

None of this is exotic. It is the difference between a broker who treats your school like a real client with real kids in it and one who treats your account like a file to renew and forget.

## The question worth sitting with

Sometime this year a student is going to get hurt at your school doing something completely ordinary, and no one is going to have done anything wrong. When that family is sitting at the kitchen table with the medical bills, what answer does your school have for them?

Is there a plan that pays toward those bills no matter whose fault it was? Do you know whether it pays first or only after the family's own insurance? Or is the honest answer that you are not sure the coverage exists, and you would only find out the day you need it most?

If you cannot answer that with confidence, you do not have a problem yet. You have a question nobody has checked. The check takes one conversation. What it costs to skip it shows up as a lawsuit you could have prevented.

---

If you want a second set of eyes on whether your student accident coverage actually matches the activities your kids are doing, that is the kind of thing we look at before anyone gets hurt.*

topics.title: Your Workers' Comp Bill Has a Credit Score Built Into It, and Nobody at Your School Has Ever Checked It for Errors
topics.subtitle: Two silent, expensive problems quietly inflate workers' comp premiums for charter schools: staff filed under the wrong job codes, and an experience modifier nobody manages. Here is how the number actually gets built, and the levers you control.

hub.content_markdown:
# Your Workers' Comp Bill Has a Credit Score Built Into It, and Nobody at Your School Has Ever Checked It for Errors

*Two silent, expensive problems quietly inflate workers' comp premiums for charter schools: staff filed under the wrong job codes, and an experience modifier nobody manages. Here is how the number actually gets built, and the levers you control.*

---

Two schools, same size, same number of teachers, same payroll. One pays a workers' comp premium that is meaningfully higher than the other. Year after year.

Nothing about the building is different. The staff aren't more accident-prone. The work is identical.

The gap is a number most school leaders have never looked at, sitting on a worksheet they've never been handed.

---

## The number nobody hands you

You sign a workers' comp renewal the same way you sign everything else that arrives once a year. You check that the premium didn't lurch upward, confirm the headcount looks about right, and move on. There's a school to run.

Underneath that premium is a calculation with three moving parts, and you control more of it than anyone has told you.

Workers' comp premium starts with payroll. Then it sorts that payroll into job classification codes, each with its own rate, because the risk of injury for someone running a table saw is not the risk for someone grading papers. Then it takes one more step that catches everyone off guard. It multiplies the whole thing by a factor built from your own past claims. That factor is the experience modification, the e-mod, and it behaves almost exactly like a credit score for your insurance.

A clean claims history earns you a mod below 1.0, a discount on every dollar of premium. A rough one pushes the mod above 1.0, a surcharge on every dollar. A credit score follows you, it compounds, and it's frequently wrong in ways nobody catches until you look. Your e-mod is no different. Most schools have never pulled the worksheet to check it.

## How the e-mod actually works

Here is the mechanic in plain terms. The rating organization that calculates your mod looks at your claims over a multi-year window, usually a few years back, and compares your actual losses against what a school of your size and type would be expected to have. Perform better than expected, you drop below 1.0. Worse than expected, you climb above it.

The part that surprises people is the direction it runs. Your e-mod is backward-looking in how it's built and forward-looking in how it's applied. Last year's claims don't just cost you last year. They get baked into a multiplier that hits next year's premium, and the year after that, for the whole time they sit inside the rating window. A bad claim is a recurring charge on every renewal until it ages out.

And it's a multiplier, not an add-on. A mod of 1.25 means you pay 25 percent more on the entire base premium, across every class code, every payroll dollar. The bigger your payroll, the more a bad mod costs you in raw dollars. For a growing school, that's real money walking out the door on autopilot.

## Problem one: your staff are filed under the wrong codes

Before the mod ever enters the picture, your payroll has to get sorted into class codes, and this is where the first quiet leak starts.

Every job at your school maps to a classification, and each classification carries its own rate. Clerical and administrative work is rated low because the injury risk is low. Maintenance, grounds, food service, and similar hands-on roles are rated higher. The logic is sound. The execution is where schools bleed money, because when a code gets assigned wrong, it tends to get assigned high, and then it never gets revisited.

Picture an office administrator coded into a higher-rated category years ago when the policy was first set up, maybe by someone who didn't know the difference and grabbed the nearest code. That mistake doesn't announce itself. It quietly charges you the higher rate on that person's salary, every year, while the renewal sails through unexamined. Multiply one or two misclassifications across a full staff and several years, and you've overpaid a sum nobody ever names, because nobody ever audits the codes.

The year-end payroll audit is supposed to true this up. In practice it usually confirms how much you paid people, not whether each person was sorted into the right bucket to begin with. The codes carry forward from last year because that's the path of least resistance, and the error rides along untouched.

## Problem two: a mod that nobody is managing

The class-code leak is steady and dumb. The e-mod leak is sneakier, because it compounds, and because most of the damage happens in places a school never thinks to look.

Three things inflate a mod that nobody is steering.

First, claims that get handled passively. A claim left to drift, with no one from the school staying in contact with the adjuster or the injured employee, tends to run longer and cost more than one that's actively managed. Longer and costlier claims feed a higher mod.

Second, no return-to-work program. When an injured employee sits at home collecting wage-replacement benefits because the school has nothing for them to do, the claim's cost climbs, and that cost lands in the mod calculation. When the same employee comes back to modified or light duty, the indemnity portion of the claim shrinks, and so does the mod hit. Return-to-work is one of the most direct levers a school has, and most schools don't have a program at all.

Third, and this is the one that quietly does the most damage, reserves that never get reviewed. When a claim is open, the insurer sets aside an estimate of what it expects the claim to ultimately cost. That estimate is the reserve. Here's what almost no school leader knows: the reserve, not just the dollars actually paid out, is what feeds your e-mod. An open claim with a fat, conservative reserve can inflate your mod even if the claim eventually settles for far less. And the mod calculation pulls those figures as of a specific date each year. If a bloated reserve is sitting on an open claim when that snapshot gets taken, the inflated number locks into your mod and rides your premium for years, long after the claim closed cheap.

Nobody challenges the reserve, because the generalist broker who placed the policy treats the renewal as a form to process, not an account to manage. They don't know your open claims. They've never called the adjuster. They couldn't tell you what's reserved on what, because they've never looked, and they're not going to start the week your mod gets calculated.

## Why the errors live for years

Both leaks survive for the same reason. The system defaults to repeating last year, and no one with a stake in your money is checking the inputs.

Class codes copy forward. Reserves go unchallenged. The mod worksheet shows up, gets filed, and never gets read. A busy broker working a renewal at volume has no incentive to audit the codes line by line or to pick a fight with an adjuster over a reserve that's sitting too high. That's slow, unglamorous work, and it doesn't help them close the next account. So it doesn't happen, and the meter keeps running on your dime.

The maddening part is that every one of these is fixable, and the fixes aren't exotic.

## The levers you actually control

**Audit your class codes.** Pull the classification assigned to every position and confirm each one matches the real work that person does. An administrator filed under a hands-on code should be corrected, and depending on the rules in play, prior overpayments may be recoverable. This is a line-by-line read, and it's worth the hour.

**Manage claims like they cost you money, because they do.** Report injuries promptly, stay in contact with the adjuster, and treat every open claim as something to drive toward closure rather than something to forget. Passive claims are expensive claims.

**Stand up a return-to-work program.** Have light-duty and modified roles defined before you need them, so an injured employee can come back productively instead of sitting home while the claim and your mod both swell. This is the highest-leverage move most schools are missing entirely.

**Review open-claim reserves before the snapshot locks them in.** Know your unit statistical date, the cutoff when claim figures get pulled into your mod. In the weeks before it, review every open claim with your broker and adjuster. If a reserve is set higher than the facts justify, that's the moment to push for it to be revised down, before it freezes into your premium for the next several years.

That's the whole job. Read the codes, work the claims, build the return-to-work plan, and check the reserves before they harden. None of it is hard. It just requires someone who treats your school like a client instead of a renewal to clear off the desk.

## The question worth sitting with

Your e-mod is a score that follows your school, compounds across renewals, and is built from inputs that nobody on your account has verified in years.

So ask yourself this. When was the last time anyone actually pulled your e-mod worksheet, walked your class codes against your real staff roster, and reviewed your open-claim reserves before they got locked into next year's premium? Or has the renewal just been processing itself, quietly charging you for errors nobody went looking for?

If you can't answer that with confidence, you don't have a workers' comp problem yet. You have a number nobody's checked. And unlike the worst-case scenarios in insurance, this one isn't waiting for a bad day to cost you. It's costing you right now, on every renewal, in an amount you've never been shown.

---

If you want a second set of eyes on your class codes and your e-mod worksheet before your next workers' comp renewal, that's the kind of thing we do before anyone asks.*

topics.title: Your Best Teacher Just Took a Job Across Town, and Your Health Plan Helped Them Decide
topics.subtitle: Why the plain health plan most charter schools hand their staff delivers slow, shallow care at a rising price, and how two upgrades give your people better care while bending the cost curve down.

hub.content_markdown:
# Your Best Teacher Just Took a Job Across Town, and Your Health Plan Helped Them Decide

*Why the plain health plan most charter schools hand their staff delivers slow, shallow care at a rising price, and how two upgrades give your people better care while bending the cost curve down.*

---

A teacher you spent two years developing puts in her notice. The district down the road offered a little more money, sure. But when she explains it, the money isn't even the headline. She says her husband waited five weeks for a primary care appointment, her kid's inhaler cost more than a car payment, and the new place just made all of that easier.

You didn't lose her on salary. You lost her on the part of the offer you never built.

---

## The lever you're not pulling

Charter schools live and die on talent. You're recruiting against districts with pension systems and deeper salary bands, and the one place you can actually compete is the total package: culture, mission, and the quality of the benefits your people use every week. Health benefits aren't a line item to that teacher. They're whether her family can see a doctor without taking a day off and gambling on the cost.

So look at what most schools actually offer. A standard fully insured health plan, renewed every year, with a network card and a phone number and a deductible that creeps up. It is mediocre care at a rising price, and your staff feels both halves of that. The visits are rushed. The appointments are weeks out. The pharmacy counter delivers a number nobody can explain. And every spring the premium climbs again.

There are two upgrades that change the actual experience your people have and the actual money your school spends. Most charter leaders have never been shown either one, for a reason I'll get to.

## Upgrade one: Direct Primary Care

Direct Primary Care, or DPC, changes how you pay for the front door of medicine.

In the normal model, every doctor visit is a billable event. The practice files a claim, codes the encounter, waits to get paid, and the whole machine rewards volume. A primary care doctor running that treadmill is carrying two thousand patients and booking seven-minute visits to keep the lights on. That's why your teacher waited five weeks and then felt rushed out the door in ten minutes.

DPC drops the billing treadmill entirely. The plan pays a flat monthly fee per enrolled member directly to a primary care practice. In exchange, that practice gives the member unlimited access: longer appointments, same-day or next-day scheduling, direct texting or messaging with the actual doctor, and a much smaller patient panel so the doctor has time to know your people. Many DPC practices run labs in-house and dispense common generic medications at their wholesale cost, so a strep test or a blood panel or a month of a basic prescription costs the member little or nothing at the point of care.

Here is the part that matters for both care and budget. When a parent can message a doctor on Tuesday morning instead of sitting in urgent care Saturday night, small problems get caught while they're small. Blood pressure gets managed before it's a heart attack. The diabetic gets coached before the ER visit. Better access on the front end quietly prevents the expensive failures on the back end.

## DPC works with your insurance, not instead of it

This is where leaders get nervous, so let me be precise. DPC is not health insurance and it does not replace it. It covers primary care, the routine and ongoing stuff. It does nothing for a specialist, a surgery, an MRI, or a hospital stay.

So a properly built program wraps a DPC membership together with an insurance plan sitting behind it for everything else. The DPC practice handles the high-frequency, low-cost care your people use constantly, the visits and the labs and the everyday prescriptions. The wrapped insurance plan stands behind it for specialists and for the catastrophic events that are the actual reason insurance exists. Pair DPC with a higher-deductible plan or an alternatively funded plan, and the rich front-end access can offset a leaner, cheaper insurance layer, because so much care never touches the insurance at all. Your people get more, and the plan can cost less. Both can be true at once.

## Upgrade two: stop overpaying for the prescriptions

Now the pharmacy counter, where some of the strangest money in American health care moves.

Your drug benefit is run by a Pharmacy Benefit Manager, a PBM. In theory the PBM is the middleman that negotiates good drug prices on your behalf. In practice, the way most traditional PBM contracts are written, the middleman profits from keeping you in the dark. Two mechanics do most of the damage.

The first is spread pricing. The PBM pays the pharmacy one price for a drug, charges your plan a higher price, and pockets the difference, the spread. You never see either number. You see only what your plan was billed, with no way to tell how much of it was the drug and how much was the markup riding on top.

The second is rebate retention. Drug manufacturers pay rebates to get their products favorable placement on the plan's covered-drug list. A meaningful share of those rebates can stay with the PBM instead of flowing back to your school and your members. Worse, because rebates reward high list prices, the system can quietly steer toward a pricier brand-name drug over a cheaper one that would have worked fine, because the expensive drug pays a bigger rebate. The incentive is pointed the wrong way, and it's pointed at your budget.

The fix is a transparent, pass-through pharmacy model. In a pass-through arrangement, the PBM charges your plan exactly what the pharmacy was paid, with a clear, disclosed administrative fee instead of a hidden spread, and rebates flow back to the plan. You see the true cost of the drugs. When the true cost is finally visible, the games stop paying, and the spend usually comes down on its own.

## Why your school doesn't already have this

None of this is reserved for giant corporations. Schools the size of yours run DPC arrangements and transparent pharmacy programs right now. The reason yours doesn't isn't cost, and it isn't eligibility. It's that nobody offered it.

Think about what your benefits broker actually does each year. They show up near renewal, hand you the same fully insured plan with a new premium stapled to it, walk you through the increase, and call it service. That is selling a renewal. It is the easy button: no design work, no pharmacy contract to dissect, no DPC practice to vet and wrap into the plan. A generalist who carries restaurants and law firms and your school in the same book of business has no reason to build any of this, and every reason to re-up what already exists.

Designing a benefit is a different job. It means looking at how your staff actually uses care, finding where the front-end access is failing them and where the pharmacy money is leaking, and assembling DPC plus a transparent Rx layer plus the right wrapped insurance into something that serves your people and your budget at the same time. That work doesn't happen by accident, and it never happens from a broker treating your school as a file to clear before the deadline.

## Before your next renewal

You don't need to become a benefits expert to find out where you stand. You need to look honestly at one thing and notice what it tells you.

So sit with this. When your best teacher weighs your offer against the district's, and her family's real experience of seeing a doctor and filling a prescription goes into that decision, is your health plan helping you keep her, or is it quietly part of why she leaves? And if you can't say for certain, who decided that the same fully insured renewal, year after year, was the best your people could be offered?

If the honest answer is that nobody ever sat down and designed the thing, then you don't have a benefits problem. You have a question nobody on your account has bothered to ask.

---

If you want to see whether Direct Primary Care and a transparent pharmacy setup would give your staff better care for less than you're spending now, that's the design work we do before anyone talks about switching a plan.*

hub.content_markdown:
# The Health Plan Where a Healthy Year Pays You Back

*A plain-English look at level-funded plans and group captives: the funding models that return surplus to the school instead of the insurer, what they actually require, and the honest risk nobody should hide from you.*

---

There's a version of your school's health plan where a healthy year ends with money coming back to your budget instead of vanishing into the insurer's profit.

It isn't exotic. It isn't only for big corporations. Schools your size run on it right now. And the reason most charter leaders have never seriously looked at it is that their generalist broker found it easier to renew the same fully insured plan every year and pass along the increase.

Here's how the alternatives actually work, who each one fits, and the part of the pitch most people leave out: the real risk.

---

## The spectrum, in plain terms

Health plan funding runs along a spectrum, and fully insured sits at one end of it.

At that end, you pay a fixed premium, the insurer takes all the risk, and the insurer keeps any surplus. Predictable, and built so you never share in a good year.

At the far other end is full self-funding, where the employer pays claims directly and keeps the savings when claims are low. It offers the most control and the most upside, but on its own it carries real volatility, and it doesn't work well for a smaller employer, because a single catastrophic claim can blow a hole in the budget. Self-funding relies on having enough covered lives to make the averages stable, and most individual schools don't have that on their own.

Between those two ends sit the two models worth a charter school's attention: level-funded plans and group captives.

## Level-funded: the step off fully insured, with a safety net

A level-funded plan is the practical first move away from fully insured, and it's built for exactly the small-to-midsize employer a charter school usually is.

You pay a set monthly amount, which keeps the budget predictability you're used to. That payment splits into three parts: a claims fund to pay your people's medical claims, the administrative fee, and the premium for stop-loss insurance. Stop-loss is the safety net. It's a policy that caps your exposure, so if claims run high, on a single large claim or across the whole group, stop-loss absorbs the overage and your downside is limited to what you already budgeted.

Now the upside that fully insured never gave you: if your group's actual claims come in below the funded amount, you get the surplus back as a refund, typically within a few months of the plan year closing. A healthy year finally pays you instead of the carrier.

There's a second benefit that's easy to overlook and genuinely valuable. Level-funded plans give you real monthly claims data, things like emergency room usage, prescription patterns, and cost drivers. For the first time you can see what's actually moving your costs and manage it, instead of being handed a renewal number with no explanation.

Two honest caveats. Level-funded plans carry more compliance and reporting responsibility than fully insured, and they aren't available in every state because of how stop-loss is regulated. A competent broker handles the first and checks the second before recommending anything.

## Group captives: for bigger schools, or schools willing to band together

A group captive is the next level of control, and it's where the model gets interesting for the charter world specifically.

A captive is an insurance company owned by the employers in it. Instead of buying coverage from a carrier that keeps the profit, a group of like-sized employers pool together and effectively insure themselves, with structure around it so no single member carries the whole risk. It usually works in three layers. Each employer self-funds its own routine, predictable claims up to a set point. Above that, a shared middle layer pools the mid-sized claims across all the member employers, so one member's rough year is cushioned by the group. Above that, reinsurance catches the truly catastrophic claims.

The payoff: a large share of what used to be fixed premium becomes variable cost, and the dollars that aren't spent on claims come back to the members as dividends rather than staying with an insurer. Members also get deep claims data, get underwritten on their own performance instead of being lumped into an opaque pool, and gain real negotiating leverage with administrators and pharmacy managers.

The charter-specific angle is the one worth sitting with. A single school may be too small to self-fund alone. But charter schools share a risk profile, and banding together into a captive is exactly the kind of pooling that lets smaller organizations reach a scale none of them could reach on their own. The model is built for groups of similar employers who want control without carrying the full risk solo.

## The honest risk, said out loud

Any broker who pitches these as free money is lying to you, so here's the straight version.

These models move some risk from the insurer onto the school. In a bad claims year, you will pay more than your best case, up to the point where stop-loss or the captive's reinsurance takes over. That ceiling is defined and known in advance, which is the whole point of the structure, but it is higher than the flat premium of a fully insured plan in a bad year. The tradeoff you're making is accepting a defined, capped downside in exchange for keeping the upside in a good year and getting data you can actually act on.

They also demand more from your broker. These plans live or die on the claims analysis, the stop-loss contract terms, and ongoing management. A broker who can't or won't do that work has no business putting you in one. That's the real reason a generalist defaults to fully insured: it requires nothing of them.

## Before you renew the same plan one more time

You don't have to commit to anything to find out whether this math works for your school. You need someone to run your actual numbers, not hand you a generic pitch.

So here's the question worth asking:

Has anyone ever run your school's real claims history against a level-funded or captive model to show you, in dollars, what a healthy year would have returned to your budget instead of the insurer's? If the honest answer is no, you've been renewing the most expensive version of this without ever seeing the alternative.

It's because renewing the same fully insured plan every year and passing along the increase is easy, and the alternatives ask the broker to actually do some work.

The charter angle is the one worth sitting with: one school may be too small to do this alone, but charter schools share a risk profile, and banding together reaches a scale none of them could reach solo.

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Fully insured isn't your only option. Before you renew, see how the funding models actually compare, including the downside.

Health plan funding runs along a spectrum, and where a charter school sits on it decides whether a healthy year returns money to the budget or stays with the insurer. Here is how the four funding models compare on the points that matter to a school: who carries the risk, who keeps the surplus, the data you get back, and the honest downside. Below the matrix is the checklist we run before recommending any change.

## The funding spectrum, model by model

| Model | Who carries the risk | Surplus in a good year | Claims data you get | The honest downside |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fully insured** | Insurer carries all of it | Insurer keeps it | Little to none; you get a renewal number | None on claims, but you never share in a healthy year |
| **Level-funded** | Shared; stop-loss caps your exposure | Returned to the school as a refund | Real monthly claims data (ER usage, Rx, cost drivers) | Pay more than best case in a bad year, up to the stop-loss ceiling; more compliance; not sold in every state |
| **Group captive** | Pooled across member employers; reinsurance on top | Returned to members as dividends | Deep data; underwritten on your own performance | Variable cost in a bad year up to the reinsurance layer; requires scale and active management |
| **Full self-funding** | Employer carries it directly | Employer keeps all of it | Full data and control | Real volatility; a single catastrophic claim can blow a hole in a smaller budget |

The two middle models, level-funded and group captive, are the ones built for the small-to-midsize employer a charter school usually is. A single school may be too small to self-fund alone, but charter schools share a risk profile, which is exactly what makes captive pooling reach a scale none of them could reach solo.

- [ ] Confirm your state allows level-funded plans (stop-loss regulation varies by state).
- [ ] Model a level-funded plan against your real claims and quantify the refund in a healthy year.
- [ ] Read the stop-loss contract terms; the plan lives or dies on them.
- [ ] Confirm the stop-loss ceiling so you know your maximum exposure in a bad year before you sign.
- [ ] If size or appetite supports it, evaluate a group captive and its three layers (self-funded, shared, reinsurance).
- [ ] Confirm you will receive monthly claims data and that someone is committed to managing it.
- [ ] Document the modeled downside, not just the upside, for the board record.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker defaults to fully insured because it requires nothing of them: renew the same plan, pass along the increase, move on. These alternatives demand real claims analysis, careful stop-loss contract review, and ongoing management. hub.content_markdown:
# Your School Had a Healthy Year. Your Reward Was a 12% Premium Increase. Here's Where the Money Went.

*Why the standard health plan most charter schools carry is built so the school loses in a good year and loses in a bad one, and what that costs a budget that can't absorb it.*

---

Your staff had a healthy year. No major surgeries, no catastrophic claims, a quiet twelve months. By any honest measure, your health plan made money.

Then renewal arrives, and the premium goes up double digits anyway.

So where did the money from your healthy year go? It went to the insurer, and you are never getting it back. That's not a glitch. That's how the plan was designed to work, and most school leaders have never had it explained to them.

---

## How a fully insured plan actually treats you

The plan most charter schools carry is called fully insured. You pay a fixed premium every month. The insurance company takes on the risk of paying claims. Simple, predictable, and on the surface it sounds fair.

Here's the part that isn't fair, and it comes down to one question: who keeps the money in a good year?

In a fully insured plan, if your people stay healthy and claims come in well below what you paid in premium, the insurer keeps the difference. All of it. The surplus your healthy workforce generated becomes the insurer's profit. You don't get a refund. You don't get a credit. You often don't even get told it happened.

Now flip it. If you have a bad year and claims run high, the insurer covers it, then comes back at renewal and raises your premium to recover the cost and protect their margin.

Read those two outcomes together and you'll see the trap. Good year, they keep the surplus. Bad year, they raise your rate. You carry the downside in both directions and capture the upside in neither. Heads they win, tails you lose.

## You're also managing the cost completely blind

There's a quieter problem stacked on top of the financial one. A fully insured plan gives you almost no usable claims data.

You can't see what's driving your costs. You don't know if it's a handful of large claims, a pharmacy spend problem, overuse of the emergency room, or something you could actually address. You're handed a renewal number and told to take it or leave it, with no window into how that number was built.

You can't manage what you can't see. Fully insured keeps you from seeing it, which conveniently keeps you dependent on the renewal the insurer hands you.

## Why this lands so hard on a school

For a school, every premium dollar is a dollar with somewhere better to be. It's a classroom aide, a counselor, a raise that keeps a good teacher from leaving for the district down the road. A double-digit increase isn't a line item you shrug off. It's a real cut somewhere else in the building.

And recruitment makes it worse. You're competing for staff against public districts that often have deep benefits and pension systems behind them. When your health plan eats your budget and still raises rates every year, you lose ground on the exact tool you'd use to attract and keep good people. The fully insured model quietly works against the thing your school most needs to do.

## There is another way the math can run

Here's what your generalist broker probably never told you: the surplus from a healthy year does not have to disappear into the insurer's pocket. There are funding models built so that a good claims year puts money back into your budget instead of theirs, and they are not reserved for giant corporations. Schools your size use them.

Those models come with their own structure and their own honest tradeoffs, which is its own conversation and its own paper. The point here is narrower and it's this: if your school is fully insured, you are in the one arrangement where you are guaranteed to never benefit from your own people's good health. You should at least know that's the deal you're in.

## What a broker who's actually working would have shown you

A broker doing the job walks you through where your premium dollars went, tells you plainly whether you had a good or bad claims year, and lays the alternative funding models on the table so you can decide with real numbers. They treat your health spend as something you can manage, not a fixed cost you're stuck absorbing.

A generalist renews you fully insured every single year because it's the easy button. No data to pull, no analysis to run, no alternatives to explain. They re-up the same plan, pass along the increase, and call it service.

## Before you accept your next renewal

You don't need a benefits degree to find out where you stand. You need to ask one question and watch how fast you get a straight answer.

So here's the one to ask:

The last time your staff had a healthy claims year, what happened to the money? Did any of it come back to your school, or did the insurer keep all of it and raise your rate anyway? If your broker can't answer that clearly, you've just learned something about both your plan and your broker.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want to see where your premium dollars actually go and what your alternatives look like in real numbers, that's the analysis we run before we ever talk about switching anything.*

topics.title: Your Teachers Spent the Whole Year Paying for Benefits They Picked in a Forty-Minute Panic
topics.subtitle: Why the once-a-year paper scramble most charter schools call open enrollment quietly wastes the benefit dollars you already spent, and what running it like a real process looks like instead.

hub.content_markdown:
# Your Teachers Spent the Whole Year Paying for Benefits They Picked in a Forty-Minute Panic

*Why the once-a-year paper scramble most charter schools call open enrollment quietly wastes the benefit dollars you already spent, and what running it like a real process looks like instead.*

---

It's the third week of a packed month. Your office manager drops a stack of forms on every teacher's desk with a sticky note and a deadline. There's one all-staff meeting, squeezed into the end of a faculty day, where a binder gets flipped through and a slide deck gets rushed.

Most of your teachers do the only rational thing a tired person does with a confusing form and a hard stop. They check the same box they checked last year, or the first box on the page, and they get back to their classroom.

Then they spend the next twelve months paying for a plan nobody helped them understand, and quietly resenting a benefit you're spending real money on.

---

## What the scramble actually costs you

A school's benefit spend is one of its three biggest line items. You are pouring real budget into health coverage, and the way most schools run enrollment guarantees a chunk of that money lands with a thud instead of a payoff.

Run it as a once-a-year fire drill and you get four predictable problems. Engagement stays low, because nobody had time to engage. Plan choices stay poor, because a teacher with a new baby and a teacher about to retire both default into the same plan they had last year. Errors pile up, because somebody is hand-keying paper forms into a carrier portal at eleven at night. And compliance exposure grows, because rushed manual data entry is exactly where eligibility mistakes, missed deadlines, and bad dependent records come from.

Put those together and you've paid full price for benefits your staff don't understand, don't value, and in some cases didn't pick on purpose. That's the real bill. Not the premium. The premium you were always going to pay. The waste is spending it and getting resentment back.

## Why the paper scramble produces bad decisions

A benefit election is a real financial decision. A teacher is choosing how a family absorbs a medical bill for the next year. That decision deserves more than a form and a deadline.

When the only support is a thick binder and a forty-minute meeting, two things happen. First, the people who need help the most, the ones with a complicated year coming, are the ones least able to sort it out from a slide. Second, defaulting feels safe even when it isn't. Last year's plan might be wrong for this year's life, but it's the choice that requires zero thinking at 4 p.m. on a Friday. So people default, and they often default into the wrong thing.

That's not a staff problem. It's a process problem. You gave people a high-stakes choice and no tools to make it.

## What modern open enrollment actually means

Run well, open enrollment stops being an event and becomes a process that runs all year. Four pieces carry it.

**Enrollment technology that feeds clean data.** A benefits administration platform replaces the paper-and-portal handoff. Employees make elections in one place, the system enforces eligibility rules as they go, and clean data flows to the carriers instead of a tired human retyping it. That single change removes a whole category of enrollment errors and the compliance risk that rides along with them.

**Decision support, not just a plan list.** Good tools walk each employee through their own situation. Expecting a baby, managing a chronic condition, rarely see a doctor, the right plan is different for each, and the platform helps them see the real tradeoff between premium, deductible, and out-of-pocket exposure for their life, not the average life. People stop defaulting because, for the first time, the choice is legible.

**Communication in multiple formats, well before the deadline.** Not one meeting. A plan. Short videos, a one-page plain-language summary, a live session, an email sequence, all landing with enough runway that a teacher can take it home and talk it over with a spouse. You meet people where they are instead of where the binder is.

**Post-enrollment education so the benefit gets used.** Signing up is the start. People need to know how to actually use what they bought, how telehealth works, what their pharmacy benefit covers, where the no-cost preventive care is. A benefit nobody uses is a benefit nobody values, and you paid for it either way.

## Why this matters more for a charter school than almost anyone

You're competing for teachers against districts that often carry deep benefits and pension systems. Benefits are one of the few levers you fully control in that fight, and a sloppy enrollment process quietly blunts it. You spend the money and your staff walk away feeling like the benefit is confusing and thin, which is the opposite of what you paid for.

You also don't have a benefits department absorbing the chaos. You have an office manager or an HR person, often wearing four other hats, doing manual data entry and chasing down correction after correction. Every error that platform would have caught is an hour that person isn't spending on the actual job. The scramble doesn't just cost staff satisfaction. It costs you the time of the person holding the whole operation together.

## Where the gap really comes from

Here's the part worth being honest about. The reason most schools still run enrollment as a paper fire drill usually traces back to the broker.

A generalist benefits broker shows up once a year, drops off a binder, maybe sits in on the one meeting, and leaves. No enrollment platform stood up. No communication plan built. No decision-support tools put in front of staff. No follow-through after the deadline to make sure anyone understands what they signed. The renewal gets processed, the same plan gets re-upped, and the broker calls it service.

That's the easy button. Dropping a binder takes an afternoon. Building a real enrollment process takes work across the whole year, and a broker who treats your school as too small to fuss over isn't going to do that work. So the school inherits the scramble, year after year, and assumes that's just how enrollment goes.

It isn't. The schools that run it well aren't bigger or richer. They just have someone who treats enrollment as a process worth building instead of a form worth dropping off.

## What it looks like when someone's actually doing the job

A broker doing the work sets up the enrollment platform so elections are clean and the data reaches carriers without anyone retyping it. They build a communication calendar that starts weeks before the deadline, in formats your staff will actually open. They put decision-support tools in front of every employee so the choice fits the person. And they come back after enrollment closes to teach people how to use what they picked.

None of that is exotic. It's the difference between a broker who treats enrollment as an annual obligation and one who treats your benefit spend as money that should actually land with your staff.

## Before your next enrollment season

You don't need to overhaul anything today. You just need to look honestly at how last year went.

So here's the question worth sitting with:

When your staff made their benefit elections last year, how many of them actually chose a plan that fit their situation, and how many just checked last year's box because nobody gave them the time, the tools, or the plain-language help to do anything else? And if you can't answer that, who on your account was supposed to make sure they could?

If that question is uncomfortable to sit with, the problem was never your teachers. It was the process they were handed, and the person who handed it to them.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want enrollment to run like a real process instead of an annual scramble, the platform, the communication, the decision support, and the follow-through, that's the kind of thing we build before your next season starts.*

topics.title: The Same Surgery Costs Your Plan Three Times What the Hospital Will Accept. You Just Paid the Big Number.
topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at reference-based pricing: how a self-funded school can pay hospital bills off a transparent benchmark instead of an inflated chargemaster, what it really saves, and the tradeoff anyone honest has to tell you about.

hub.content_markdown:
# The Same Surgery Costs Your Plan Three Times What the Hospital Will Accept. You Just Paid the Big Number.

*A plain-English look at reference-based pricing: how a self-funded school can pay hospital bills off a transparent benchmark instead of an inflated chargemaster, what it really saves, and the tradeoff anyone honest has to tell you about.*

---

A staff member has surgery. The hospital bills your plan a number with a lot of zeros. Your plan pays it, minus the discount your network negotiated, and everyone moves on.

Now picture the same procedure at the same hospital, billed to Medicare. The hospital accepts a fraction of what your plan just paid. Same operating room, same surgeon, same supplies. A wildly different price, and the lower one still keeps the lights on at the hospital.

Your plan paid the big number. Most school leaders have never been shown there was another one.

---

## What you're actually paying for in a network plan

The health plan most schools carry pays providers through a PPO network. The pitch is simple and it sounds great. The network negotiates a discount off the hospital's billed charges, and your plan pays the discounted amount.

The problem hides in the words "billed charges." A hospital's list price lives in a document called the chargemaster, and chargemaster prices are not tied to what care costs to deliver. They are set high on purpose. A 40 percent or 50 percent discount off a number that was inflated to begin with can still land well above what the same service is worth.

So the network discount, the thing that feels like the win, is a discount off a price nobody should be paying in the first place. You can negotiate hard against a fake number and still overpay. That is the quiet design of network pricing, and it is the single biggest line in your health plan: hospital and facility costs.

## Reference-based pricing flips the starting point

Reference-based pricing, usually shortened to RBP, attacks that problem at its root. Instead of starting from the hospital's inflated list price and discounting down, the plan starts from a transparent, objective benchmark and pays up from there.

The benchmark most RBP plans use is Medicare. Medicare already calculates, service by service, a defined reimbursement rate that hospitals accept every day. RBP takes that rate and pays a set multiple of it. A plan might reimburse, for example, a fixed percentage above Medicare for a given procedure, commonly somewhere in the range of 120 to 200 percent of the Medicare rate depending on the plan design and the service.

Sit with the gap for a second. Industry analyses of commercial network pricing frequently put it at several times Medicare, sometimes 300 percent or more. An RBP plan anchored at, say, 150 percent of Medicare is paying the hospital well above its Medicare rate, which is a fair and sustainable price, while paying far less than the network would have. The savings come from anchoring payment to an objective cost basis instead of an opaque list price.

That is the whole idea. You stop arguing about the discount and start defining the price.

## What this can actually save

Be careful with savings numbers, because vendors love to wave around the best-case figure. Spoken honestly: published industry estimates commonly describe self-funded employers cutting total health plan spend by something in the range of 20 to 30 percent after moving to a well-run RBP arrangement. Your school's actual result depends on your claims, your region, and how the program is run. Treat any single percentage as a marketing number until someone runs your real data.

The reason the savings can be that large is simple. Hospital and facility spend is the heaviest line in almost every health plan. Cut the price basis on the heaviest line and the whole plan moves. Trimming around the edges of a network plan never touches that.

## This only works on a self-funded chassis

RBP is not a plan you bolt onto a fully insured policy. It belongs on a self-funded or level-funded plan, the same chassis covered in the funding conversation. The reason is structural. In a self-funded arrangement, the plan, meaning your school, decides what it pays for each claim. That authority is exactly what RBP uses. The plan defines its allowable amount off the Medicare benchmark, and a third-party administrator processes claims and pays providers at that defined amount.

If you are still fully insured, the insurer sets the price and keeps the upside, so there is no lever for you to pull. RBP is one of the concrete reasons the move off fully insured matters. It opens a door that stays locked otherwise.

## Now the tradeoff, said out loud

Anyone selling RBP as free money is not being straight with you, so here is the honest part.

Because an RBP plan pays off a benchmark instead of a signed network contract, there is no contract stopping a hospital from sending the member a bill for the difference between its list price and what the plan paid. That is called balance billing, and it is the real risk of this model. It is the thing the slick pitch decks gloss over.

This risk is manageable, and a serious RBP program manages it as a core feature, not an afterthought. Good programs build in member advocacy and balance-bill defense. When a provider sends a balance bill, a patient advocacy and legal team steps in on the member's behalf, engages the provider, and works to resolve the charge so the employee is not left holding it. Reputable administrators report that the large majority of claims end with no balance bill to the member, but "large majority" is not "never," and you should distrust anyone who promises never.

There are two more real frictions. Some providers, particularly large hospital systems, push back on RBP and may be less willing to treat plan members smoothly or may require more coordination up front. And the whole thing depends on employee communication. Your staff need to understand the plan before they get a surprising bill in the mail, and they need to know exactly who to call the moment one shows up. An RBP plan with weak member support is a complaint factory. An RBP plan with strong advocacy is a cost-control engine your staff barely notice.

## Why your school was never shown this

Here is the pattern. Reference-based pricing is more work to design, more work to explain to staff, and more work to support once it is running. It requires a broker who will sit with your people, set up the advocacy and defense services correctly, and stay in the room when a member calls about a bill.

A generalist benefits broker does none of that. The easy-button renewal is to re-up the same network plan, pass along the increase, and move on to the next account. RBP never comes up, not because it would not fit your school, but because it would ask something of the broker. So the heaviest, most attackable line in your entire health plan goes unexamined year after year, and you keep paying the big number while a fair number was sitting right there.

That is not a knock on every broker. It is a description of what happens when nobody on your account treats your health spend as something to actively manage.

## Before your next renewal

You do not need to commit to anything to find out whether this fits. You need one honest look at where your money goes.

So here is the question worth sitting with:

On your largest hospital and facility claims last year, has anyone ever shown you what those services would have cost benchmarked against Medicare, next to what your plan actually paid? If no one has ever put those two numbers side by side for you, how would you know whether you have a cost problem, or just a broker who never looked?

If you cannot answer that, you do not have a verdict yet. You have a question nobody on your account has bothered to ask on your behalf.

---

If you want to see what your real hospital claims would have cost on a transparent benchmark, with the balance-billing risk explained straight and the member-advocacy piece built in, that's the analysis we run before recommending a single change.*
